proposals.title: Beyond Policy Prescription: Grassroots Negotiation of Integration and Belonging Among Middle-Class Chinese Immigrant Families in Singapore
proposals.motivation: In Singapore, a major immigrant-receiving society, integration is predominantly framed through state-led bilingual education and multicultural policies. However, pre-2026 research, including Yang's work, reveals significant tensions and anxieties among middle-class Chinese immigrant parents regarding their children's academic and social belonging. This highlights a critical gap in understanding how integration is actively managed outside formal policy frameworks, from the bottom up.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore engage in a repertoire of grassroots, community-based strategies to navigate integration challenges, and that these informal practices are more responsive to daily tensions and more consequential for subjective belonging than state-level policy adherence alone.
proposals.method: This qualitative study employs a multi-sited ethnographic design and participatory inquiry methods. We will conduct in-depth, longitudinal interviews (n=40-50) and participant observation with parent networks in community centers, religious institutions, and informal tutoring co-ops. Data analysis will utilize a grounded theory approach, facilitated by NVivo software, to code for emergent themes of 'community-led solutioning,' contrasting these findings with parents' stated engagement with official state integration platforms.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a nuanced, agent-centric model of immigrant integration that complements top-down policy analyses. It will empirically document the specific forms of social capital and community resource mobilization used by a key demographic. The findings will challenge the primacy of institutional solutions in migration literature, offering new insights for scholars of education, transnationalism, and urban sociology.
proposals.novelty: While existing work documents parental anxieties and policy critiques, this project uniquely shifts the analytical focus to the proactive, collective strategies parents devise independently. It moves beyond diagnosing challenges to mapping the grassroots ecosystem of integration practices that operate parallel to, and sometimes in tension with, state-led systems.

proposals.title: Navigating the 'Perfect' System: An Inquiry into the Strategic Challenges and Anxieties of Middle-Class Singaporean Parents in the Local Education Landscape
proposals.motivation: Existing research on Singapore's high-performing but complex education system often frames immigrant parents as lacking cultural and institutional knowledge, leading to anxiety. This overlooks the significant challenges and strategic navigation required by local parents who, despite possessing this assumed 'perfect' system knowledge, still face intense pressures. Understanding their struggles is crucial for a complete sociological picture of educational inequality and parenting stress in a globally competitive city-state.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that middle-class Singaporean parents, despite deep familiarity with the local education system, experience significant strategic challenges and class anxieties, employing complex, context-dependent navigation strategies that are inconsistent and evolve with systemic changes.
proposals.method: This qualitative study will employ a comparative, longitudinal inquiry design. We will conduct in-depth, semi-structured interviews and ethnographic observations with 30-40 middle-class Singaporean parents over 18 months, tracking their navigation of key educational milestones (e.g., Primary 1 registration, PSLE). Data analysis will utilize a grounded theory approach, facilitated by NVivo software, to code for themes of strategy, anxiety, and system knowledge. This will be complemented by a critical discourse analysis of local parenting forums and policy documents to contextualize parental narratives within broader public discourses.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a critical inversion of the 'immigrant deficit' frame, demonstrating that systemic complexity creates universal challenges, not just for newcomers. It will expand the literature on intensive parenting and class reproduction by showing how local cultural capital is dynamically deployed and can be a source of stress. The findings will offer a more nuanced understanding of educational policy impacts on all families in Singapore.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in shifting the analytical lens from the presumed knowledge gap of immigrants to the strategic dilemmas of the 'knowledgeable' local, revealing that perfect system knowledge is a myth and that navigation itself is a core site of anxiety and class performance. This directly contrasts with and complements Yang's existing work on Chinese immigrant parents.

proposals.title: Systemic Novices or Cultural Outsiders? Disentangling New-Parent and Immigrant Status in the Educational Challenges of Middle-Class Chinese Families in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Research on immigrant parenting often conflates challenges arising from unfamiliarity with a new education system with those stemming from a minority immigrant status. In Singapore's complex, high-stakes bilingual and streaming system, this conflation obscures targeted policy and support mechanisms. Pre-2026 work by Yang and others on Chinese immigrant parents' anxieties sets the stage to empirically separate these two dimensions of 'newness'.
proposals.hypothesis: The primary challenges reported by middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in navigating Singapore's education system are more strongly correlated with being a novice parent within that specific system than with their status as ethnic Chinese immigrants. We hypothesize that longitudinal data will show these challenges converge with those of new, non-immigrant Singaporean parents over time.
proposals.method: This study employs a longitudinal, comparative qualitative design using narrative inquiry and focused ethnography. We will conduct in-depth, semi-structured interviews and observational diaries over three years with three matched cohorts: (1) middle-class Chinese immigrant parents with children entering Primary 1, (2) middle-class Singaporean-born Chinese parents with their first child entering Primary 1, and (3) experienced Singaporean-born Chinese parents with a younger child entering Primary 1. Data will be analyzed using iterative thematic analysis and discourse analysis, with specific attention to coding for 'system navigation' versus 'cultural identity' challenges.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel theoretical framework for distinguishing systemic from immigrant-specific parental challenges. It will offer empirical evidence to refine Singapore's parent support programs, moving beyond broad 'immigrant integration' models to address specific informational and navigational deficits faced by all new parents. The findings will contribute to international scholarship on parenting and education by decoupling ethnicity/immigration from institutional unfamiliarity.
proposals.novelty: While existing literature, including Yang's prior work, focuses on the immigrant experience as a monolithic source of anxiety, this study's novelty lies in its controlled, longitudinal comparison that treats 'new parent in the system' as a distinct analytical category from 'immigrant parent'. This directly tests the assumed primacy of immigrant status in shaping educational engagement.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Efficacy of Coping Strategies: A Longitudinal Analysis of Chinese Immigrant Parents' Practices and Child Academic Outcomes in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore navigate a high-stakes, competitive educational landscape while managing transnational anxieties and a bilingual policy that often marginalizes their mother tongue. Existing qualitative work by Yang and others reveals a complex repertoire of coping strategies, from 'tiger' parenting to strategic school choice, but their relative effectiveness in improving concrete child outcomes remains empirically untested. This gap limits evidence-based guidance for parents and policymakers in a pre-2026 context of intense educational stratification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that parental strategies emphasizing structured cultural brokerage and adaptive, child-responsive academic support will show a stronger positive correlation with children's longitudinal academic performance and socio-emotional well-being than strategies focused solely on academic intensification or reactive anxiety management.
proposals.method: We will employ a mixed-methods longitudinal design. First, a quantitative panel survey of 300 Chinese immigrant families will track parenting practices, child academic grades (PSLE/secondary), and well-being metrics over three years. We will use Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) with latent growth curves to model the impact of strategy clusters on outcome trajectories. Second, embedded comparative case studies (n=20 families) using ethnographic interviews and diary methods will provide mechanistic insights into the 'how' and 'why' behind the quantitative correlations, following an explanatory sequential design.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal, quantitative evidence linking specific parental coping strategies to measurable child outcomes in Singapore's immigrant context, moving beyond discourse analysis. It will contribute a validated framework for 'effective strategic adaptation' to the fields of transnational migration and education sociology, offering actionable insights for family support programs and bridging the qualitative-quantitative divide in parenting research.
proposals.novelty: While prior work descriptively catalogs parenting discourses and anxieties, this project innovates by empirically testing their causal efficacy on outcomes, combining robust longitudinal modeling with deep qualitative inquiry to explain the mechanisms of effective strategy implementation.

proposals.title: Navigating the Knowledge Ecosystem: A Social Network Analysis of Information Flows Among Middle-Class Chinese Immigrant Parents in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore face significant challenges navigating a complex, high-stakes education system, as evidenced by pre-2026 research on their transnational anxieties and inconsistent parenting discourses. Their access to and trust in information about schooling, language policy, and university admissions critically shapes their children's outcomes and their own sense of security. Understanding the structure and efficacy of their information networks is essential for identifying systemic knowledge gaps that perpetuate inequality and stress.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the information networks of these parents are fragmented and stratified, leading to systematic knowledge gaps, particularly regarding the nuances of Singapore's bilingual education policy and long-term academic pathways. Further, we posit that reliance on transnational (China-based) versus local (Singapore-based) network clusters correlates with distinct types of informational deficits and parenting strategies.
proposals.method: We will employ a mixed-methods social network analysis (SNA). Using ego-centric network surveys with a purposive sample of 50-70 parents, we will map nodes (sources like schools, tutors, online forums, family) and ties (frequency, trust, content type). Quantitative analysis will use UCINET software to calculate network metrics (density, centrality, homophily). This will be integrated with qualitative data from follow-up interviews, using thematic analysis to interpret how network structures create and reinforce specific challenges, such as misunderstandings of Mother Tongue language requirements.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first structural map of information ecosystems for this demographic, moving beyond discourse analysis to model how knowledge actually circulates. It will empirically identify critical bottlenecks and unreliable network nodes that create actionable knowledge gaps. The findings will contribute to more targeted policy and community support, helping institutions bridge these gaps effectively.
proposals.novelty: While existing work analyzes parental discourse and anxieties, this study innovatively applies formal SNA—a method common in sociology but underused in this context—to objectively model the information infrastructure itself. It uniquely links macro network structures to micro-level knowledge challenges and parenting behaviors identified in prior qualitative studies.

proposals.title: Co-Designing Navigation: A Human-Centered Design Approach to Support Tools for Middle-Class Chinese Immigrant Parents in Singapore's Education System
proposals.motivation: Middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore face complex, transnational anxieties navigating a competitive education system and a bilingual policy that often marginalizes their mother tongue. Pre-2026 research by Yang and others documents their inconsistent, reluctant, yet intensive parenting strategies, highlighting a critical need for support. Existing resources are often top-down; a participatory, design-led approach is needed to create tools that genuinely address their lived challenges of cultural negotiation and systemic navigation.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a structured human-centered design (HCD) process to co-create digital or physical navigation aids with immigrant parents will yield support tools that are more contextually relevant, culturally resonant, and effective in reducing parental anxiety and improving engagement with Singapore's education system than expert-driven solutions.
proposals.method: This study will employ a multi-phase, qualitative HCD methodology. First, in-depth ethnographic inquiry and participatory workshops will map parent journeys and identify key pain points (e.g., school choice, mother-tongue support). Second, using iterative prototyping cycles (e.g., paper prototypes, wireframes), parents will co-design tool concepts. Third, specific design architectures will be tested, such as a decision-tree algorithm for school selection pathways or a community-sourced, wiki-style platform for sharing localized strategies. The process emphasizes continuous feedback loops and adaptation to the international, class-based contexts of the participants.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a novel, empirically grounded framework for developing immigrant parent support tools through co-design. It will advance sociological understanding of how design thinking can mediate structural challenges in education, moving beyond descriptive analysis to intervention. The resulting prototypes and process model will offer actionable insights for educators, policymakers, and family support services in multicultural, immigrant-receiving cities.
proposals.novelty: While prior work meticulously documents parental discourses and anxieties, this project innovates by actively engaging parents as design partners to create tangible solutions, bridging sociological inquiry with engineering design praxis. It applies the concrete, iterative methods of HCD—common in product development but rare in studies of immigrant family strategies—to the specific socio-educational challenges of this population.

proposals.title: Navigating the Dual Burden: A Study of Child Brokers in Middle-Class Chinese Immigrant Families in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Research on immigrant families in Singapore has extensively documented parental strategies for navigating education and social systems, but largely from the parents' perspective. This leaves a critical gap in understanding the active, dual role children play as they navigate these systems for their own development while also acting as cultural and linguistic brokers for their parents. In the pre-2026 context of Singapore's competitive education landscape and its specific bilingual policy, understanding this dual navigation is key to comprehending family adaptation and intergenerational dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that children in middle-class Chinese immigrant families in Singapore develop distinct, context-dependent navigation strategies, acting as 'system navigators' for their own educational trajectories and as 'family brokers' for their parents, and that this dual role creates unique psychosocial pressures and competencies that vary by the child's age, schooling stage, and family's length of residence.
proposals.method: This qualitative study will employ a longitudinal, multi-method design. We will conduct semi-structured interviews, child-led photo-elicitation exercises, and observational diaries with 25-30 child-parent dyads over 18 months. Data analysis will utilize a constructivist grounded theory approach, facilitated by NVivo software, to iteratively code for themes of navigation strategy, brokerage activity, and emotional labor. This will be complemented by discourse analysis of parent-child conversations about school and bureaucracy to examine interactional dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a child-centric framework to the literature on immigrant adaptation, shifting the focus from parents as sole agents to children as co-navigators. It will provide empirical evidence on how children's brokerage influences family capital and their own identity formation. The findings will offer nuanced insights for Singaporean educators and policymakers working with immigrant families on issues of language support and social integration.
proposals.novelty: While existing work examines parental anxieties or children's academic adjustment, this study uniquely conceptualizes the child's experience as a dual problem of self-navigation and parental support. It moves beyond viewing children as passive recipients of parenting strategies to active agents managing two complex systems simultaneously.

proposals.title: Institutional Stratification and Pedagogical Practice: A Comparative Study of Issue Investigation (II) Enactment in Singapore's Express and Normal Academic Streams
proposals.motivation: Singapore's education system is characterized by academic streaming, which creates distinct institutional cultures and student cohorts. Pre-2026 research on middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore reveals their acute class anxieties and strategic navigation of the system, but has not examined how school-level structures shape the implementation of key curricular reforms like the humanities' Issue Investigation (II) task. Understanding this is crucial, as differential implementation may reinforce educational inequalities that immigrant and local families seek to navigate.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the enactment of the Issue Investigation pedagogy will differ systematically between Express and Normal Academic (NA) streams, with Express stream classrooms exhibiting a culture of inquiry more aligned with the reform's ideals, while NA stream classrooms demonstrate a more structured, teacher-directed approach due to differing institutional priorities, resource allocations, and perceived student readiness.
proposals.method: The study will employ a comparative ethnographic case study design across four secondary schools (two with Express, two with NA focus). Data collection will include: 1) participant observation of II lessons and teacher meetings; 2) in-depth interviews with humanities teachers and school leaders; and 3) documentary analysis of II project guidelines and student work. Analysis will follow a thematic approach using NVivo software, employing constant comparative methods to identify patterns in pedagogical discourse, task framing, and assessment practices across stream contexts.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a meso-level institutional analysis to the literature on immigrant parenting and education in Singapore, moving beyond family strategies to show how school structures mediate educational experiences. It will provide empirical evidence on how streaming influences inquiry-based learning, offering critical insights for policymakers and educators on equity in curricular implementation.
proposals.novelty: While existing work details parental anxieties about streaming, no study has ethnographically compared the *in-class pedagogical enactment* of a common reform across streams. This shifts the focus from family discourse to institutional practice, uniquely linking the sociology of streaming with the anthropology of educational policy implementation.

proposals.title: Heterogeneity in the Field: A Typology of Educational Strategies Among Middle-Class Chinese Immigrant Parents in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Research on immigrant parenting often treats 'immigrant parents' as a monolithic group, assuming shared strategies and anxieties based on their minority status. This obscures significant internal variation and limits the explanatory power of sociological and educational analyses. In the pre-2026 context of Singapore's competitive education system and its specific bilingual policy, understanding this diversity is crucial for policymakers and educators to design effective support.
proposals.hypothesis: Middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore are not a homogeneous group but can be categorized into distinct, internally coherent typologies based on their educational strategies, which are shaped by the intersection of pre-migration cultural capital, length of residence, and perceived pathways to security in Singaporean society.
proposals.method: This study employs a mixed-methods, comparative case study design. Following a 'challenges and inquiry' framework, we will conduct in-depth, semi-structured interviews with 40-50 parents, stratified by length of residence (new arrivals vs. long-term settlers) and child's educational stage. Data analysis will use a two-stage approach: first, iterative thematic coding to identify core strategic dimensions (e.g., mother-tongue prioritization, shadow education use, transnational hedging); second, application of Qualitative Comparative Analysis (QCA) to construct a typology based on necessary and sufficient conditions for each strategic cluster, moving beyond descriptive categories to configurational causality.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a nuanced, empirically grounded typology that challenges the homogeneous analytical category of 'immigrant parent.' It will advance the field of transnational education studies by demonstrating how class and immigrant status interact dynamically over time. The findings will offer concrete insights for Singapore's education ministry on the varied needs within the Chinese immigrant community regarding bilingual policy and school-parent partnerships.
proposals.novelty: While existing work notes inconsistencies in parenting discourse, this study systematically maps the strategic heterogeneity and identifies the specific configurations of factors that produce it. The application of QCA to qualitative parenting data represents a novel methodological integration for this field, moving from observing variation to explaining its causal logic.

proposals.title: Discursive Dissonance and Educational Action: A Longitudinal Mixed-Methods Analysis of Middle-Class Chinese Immigrant Parents in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Prior research on immigrant parenting often treats articulated ideologies as direct predictors of action, yet Peidong Yang's own work reveals significant inconsistencies in parental discourse. In the pre-2026 context of Singapore's high-stakes, globally competitive education system, understanding the precise conditions under which parental talk translates into concrete educational choices is critical for policy and for theorizing the immigrant family as a site of strategic adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the translation of parental educational discourse into action is not direct but is mediated by specific, temporally-bound challenges related to Singapore's institutional landscape (e.g., school placement pressures, Mother Tongue Language policy) and is characterized by strategic, context-aware deviations from stated ideologies.
proposals.method: We propose a three-year longitudinal, mixed-methods inquiry. We will recruit 40 middle-class Chinese immigrant families in Singapore. Data collection involves: 1) Annual semi-structured interviews analyzed via iterative, inductive-deductive thematic coding to map discursive shifts; 2) Annual quantitative surveys tracking concrete educational investments (tutoring, enrichment, school choice); 3) A discourse-action dissonance index, calculated using a custom algorithm based on Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) topic modeling of interview transcripts and correlating derived topic prevalence with survey-reported actions. This triangulates qualitative patterns with quantified measures of inconsistency.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a dynamic, process-oriented model of immigrant parenting, moving beyond static snapshots of ideology. It will empirically identify the institutional 'pressure points' in Singapore that trigger discursive adjustments and strategic actions, offering concrete insights for educators and policymakers engaging with immigrant communities.
proposals.novelty: While existing work notes parental anxieties, this study uniquely inverts the causal assumption from 'discourse drives action' to treating discourse and action as co-constituted and often divergent, analyzing their relationship longitudinally with a novel mixed-methods analytical framework.

proposals.title: Beyond the Dichotomy: A Comparative Inquiry into the Academic Rigor and Parental Challenges in Singapore's Local and International School Tracks
proposals.motivation: Middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore often navigate a perceived binary between academically stressful local schools and less rigorous international schools, a key theme in pre-2026 literature on their transnational class anxieties and parenting strategies. This oversimplified dichotomy obscures the complex realities of both educational tracks and limits our understanding of how parents make strategic choices in a competitive, globalized field. Challenging this assumption is crucial for a nuanced analysis of immigrant educational decision-making and its implications for social reproduction.
proposals.hypothesis: The perceived dichotomy between uniformly rigorous local schools and uniformly relaxed international schools is empirically false; significant variation in academic demands and parental challenges exists within both tracks, shaped by specific school cultures, curricula, and parental social capital.
proposals.method: This study employs a comparative, multi-method design. First, a critical discourse analysis of school websites, prospectuses, and public exam data (e.g., IB, IGCSE, PSLE) will deconstruct institutional narratives of rigor. Second, semi-structured interviews and ethnographic observations in 3-4 contrasting schools (e.g., a high-pressure international IB school, a local 'neighborhood' school) will capture lived experiences. Third, thematic analysis, informed by Bourdieusian concepts of capital and field, will be applied to interview transcripts and field notes to identify patterns of challenge and strategy.
proposals.contribution: It will provide a more granular, empirically grounded map of Singapore's educational landscape, moving beyond stereotypes. The research will contribute to theories of immigrant parenting by showing how class strategies are adapted to a complex, multi-track system, and offer insights for policymakers on the diverse pressures facing families in different school environments.
proposals.novelty: While existing work documents parents' perceptions of the dichotomy, this study directly tests and challenges it through systematic comparison of both tracks, shifting the focus from parental discourse to the institutional and experiential realities of academic rigor.

proposals.title: Cognitive Maps of Educational Success: Visualizing the Ideological Schemas of Middle-Class Chinese Immigrant Parents in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Prior research on middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore reveals complex, often contradictory, parenting ideologies shaped by transnational class anxieties and Singapore's bilingual education system. Understanding the internal structure and connections within these belief systems is crucial, as they directly influence parenting practices and children's educational outcomes. Pre-2026, qualitative analysis has described these tensions, but a method to systematically model and visualize the cognitive architecture of parental ideologies is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cognitive mapping will reveal distinct, internally structured schemas of 'a good education' among these parents, and that these schemas will vary systematically based on factors like length of residence in Singapore and perceived social mobility trajectory.
proposals.method: We will conduct in-depth, semi-structured interviews with 30-40 middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore, focusing on their educational goals, strategies, and challenges. Interview transcripts will be analyzed using established cognitive mapping techniques from psychology and decision sciences. Specifically, we will employ causal mapping procedures to extract key concepts (e.g., 'bilingualism', 'elite school', 'holistic development', 'future security') and map their perceived causal relationships. The analysis will use software like Decision Explorer or NVivo for coding and visualization, followed by cluster and centrality analyses to identify core and peripheral beliefs within each cognitive map.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, visual model of the belief systems driving intensive parenting in a transnational context, moving beyond descriptive discourse analysis. It will contribute methodologically to the sociology of education and migration by importing and adapting a rigorous cognitive science tool. The findings will offer concrete insights for educators and policymakers seeking to engage with the nuanced expectations of immigrant families.
proposals.novelty: While existing work documents parental narratives, this study is the first to apply formal cognitive mapping to systematically diagram and analyze the structure of educational ideologies among this population. It transforms qualitative descriptions into testable, visual models of causal belief systems.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Trade-offs: A Conjoint Analysis of School Choice Preferences Among Middle-Class Chinese Immigrant Parents in Singapore
proposals.motivation: School choice is a critical site of intensive parenting and class reproduction for immigrant families, yet existing qualitative studies of Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore reveal complex, often inconsistent, decision-making logics. A systematic, quantitative understanding of how these parents actually weigh competing school attributes (e.g., academic reputation, mother-tongue support, distance, peer demographics) against each other is lacking. Applying a method from marketing can empirically uncover the hidden 'utility functions' driving their educational strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore will demonstrate a non-linear preference structure where academic reputation is not the sole dominant attribute; instead, its importance is significantly moderated by the availability of specific cultural-linguistic supports and perceptions of social belonging within the school environment.
proposals.method: We will employ a choice-based conjoint (CBC) analysis using Sawtooth Software or a comparable discrete choice modeling platform. Attributes and levels (e.g., school type: SAP vs. non-SAP; Chinese language program strength: high/medium/low; travel time: 15/30/45 mins; perceived ethnic mix) will be derived from prior qualitative findings. Participants will complete an online survey presenting randomized profiles of hypothetical schools, forcing trade-off choices. Hierarchical Bayes (HB) estimation will be used to calculate part-worth utilities and relative importance scores at both aggregate and individual segment levels.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative model of the school choice calculus for this population, moving beyond discursive analysis to reveal the implicit weights assigned to different factors. It will contribute to theories of transnational parenting by empirically testing how 'security' and cultural capital are operationalized in concrete trade-offs. The findings will also offer evidence-based insights for Singapore's education policymakers regarding the alignment of school offerings with immigrant family priorities.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the methodological transplantation of conjoint analysis—a staple in consumer research—to the sociological study of immigrant educational strategies, creating a bridge between marketing science and the anthropology of parenting. This allows for a rigorous decomposition of decision-making that qualitative interviews alone cannot achieve.

proposals.title: Temporal Logics of Security: A Time-Use Diary Study of Educational Labour in Middle-Class Chinese Immigrant Families in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Prior research on middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore reveals complex, often contradictory, parenting discourses and anxieties about class reproduction and educational security. However, these studies rely on retrospective interviews and surveys, which can miss the granular, daily realities and emotional labour of educational investment. A detailed, real-time accounting is needed to understand how transnational class anxieties are enacted in the temporal organization of family life.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the 'security work' of these parents manifests not just in discourse but in a distinct, intensive temporal architecture of educational activities, characterized by high density, strategic sequencing, and gendered divisions of labour, which varies significantly across weekdays and weekends.
proposals.method: We will employ a mixed-methods time-use diary approach. Approximately 30-40 parent dyads from the studied demographic will complete structured electronic diaries for one weekday and one weekend day, logging all child-focused educational activities (e.g., homework supervision, tuition, language practice) in 15-minute intervals, alongside brief emotion/reflection tags. This will be complemented by a short socio-demographic survey. Data analysis will involve quantitative time-use analysis (calculating activity densities, durations, and sequences using STATA or R) and qualitative thematic analysis of reflection entries, following an abductive, iterative coding process to link temporal patterns to discursive themes of security and anxiety from prior work.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first fine-grained empirical map of the 'educational time economy' within Chinese immigrant families in Singapore, moving beyond discourse to practice. It will contribute to sociological understandings of intensive parenting by quantifying and qualifying its temporal demands, and to migration studies by showing how class strategies are temporally embedded. The diary data will offer a robust evidence base for policymakers regarding family support and educational pressure.
proposals.novelty: While time-use studies exist in family sociology, applying this method specifically to the educational labour of a transnational migrant group, and integrating it with the theoretical framework of 'doing security,' is novel. It captures the dynamic, processual nature of parenting strategies that retrospective methods may obscure.

proposals.title: Navigating the 'PSLE Crucible': A Longitudinal, Multi-Sited Inquiry into Middle-Class Chinese Immigrant Parental Discourse and Strategy During Singapore's Primary School Leaving Examination
proposals.motivation: The Primary School Leaving Examination (PSLE) is a pivotal, high-stakes transition in Singapore's education system, directly shaping a child's future academic trajectory and perceived life chances. For middle-class Chinese immigrant parents, navigating this system involves reconciling transnational educational aspirations with the intense local pressures of Singapore's meritocratic, yet highly stratified, schooling landscape. Understanding their discourse and strategic actions during this critical period reveals how transnational class anxieties are concretely enacted and how parenting ideologies are stress-tested under systemic pressure.
proposals.hypothesis: Middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore will exhibit a strategic, context-dependent shift in parenting discourse and practice during the PSLE year, moving from a previously expressed 'reluctant tiger parent' stance to a more instrumental, security-oriented approach that actively leverages both transnational cultural capital and local institutional knowledge to optimize their child's outcomes.
proposals.method: This study employs a longitudinal, multi-sited qualitative design, combining serial, in-depth phenomenological interviews with a cohort of 25-30 parent pairs over the 12-month PSLE period. It integrates digital ethnography of parent-focused social media groups (e.g., Facebook, WeChat) to capture real-time discourse. Analysis will utilize a hybrid approach: initial thematic coding (using NVivo) informed by concepts of 'concerted cultivation' and 'transnational capital,' followed by critical discourse analysis to examine linguistic shifts and the construction of 'crisis' and 'strategy.' The multi-sited method connects private parental narratives with public, community-shared discourses.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a granular, processual understanding of how a major educational transition point acts as a catalyst for specific immigrant parenting strategies, moving beyond broad characterizations of 'tiger parenting.' It will contribute to sociological theories of transnational class reproduction by showing how global middle-class anxieties are locally operationalized during high-stakes assessments. The findings will also offer concrete insights for Singaporean educators and policymakers on the lived experiences of immigrant families within a key pressure point of the national system.
proposals.novelty: While existing work examines general parenting discourses among this group, this proposal uniquely zooms in on a defined, high-pressure temporal window (the PSLE year) using a longitudinal and multi-sited digital-physical methodology to capture dynamic, real-time strategic pivots, offering a more nuanced view of ideology-in-action under systemic duress.

proposals.title: Beyond the Home: A Mixed-Methods Study of Peer, School, and Media Influence on the Identity Formation of Middle-Class Chinese Immigrant Children in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Research on immigrant families, particularly in the Singaporean context, has heavily focused on parents as the primary agents of cultural transmission and identity formation. This overlooks the complex ecosystem of influences children navigate daily. In a globalized, digitally connected city-state like Singapore, understanding the relative weight of peer networks, school systems, and digital media is crucial for developing effective educational and social integration policies.
proposals.hypothesis: For middle-class Chinese immigrant children in Singapore, peer networks and school-based experiences exert a stronger influence on their evolving cultural and academic identities than parental strategies, especially as children progress through the education system. Parental influence becomes more contested and negotiated within these other domains.
proposals.method: This study employs a longitudinal, sequential mixed-methods design. First, a quantitative survey of 200+ children (aged 10-15) and their parents will measure perceived influence levels across domains (parents, peers, school, media) on identity markers. This will be analyzed using structural equation modeling to map influence pathways. Second, based on survey clusters, 30-40 children will participate in qualitative digital ethnography (analysis of social media footprints) and photo-elicitation interviews, using participant-generated images of 'influences' to guide in-depth discussion. School-level discourse analysis of policy texts and classroom interactions will provide institutional context.
proposals.contribution: It will provide a more nuanced, child-centric model of immigrant identity formation, challenging the parent-centric paradigm. The findings will offer concrete insights for Singaporean educators and policymakers on how to leverage peer and school environments to support positive identity development, complementing existing family-focused support structures.
proposals.novelty: While existing work scrutinizes parental discourse, this study directly centers the child's perspective and empirically compares the influence of multiple, concurrent social spheres. It innovatively combines quantitative influence mapping with digital ethnography and visual methods to capture the lived, multi-sited experience of identity negotiation.

proposals.title: Negotiating Extremes: A Comparative Case Study of Heritage Rejection and Integration Resistance among Middle-Class Chinese Immigrant Families in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Research on immigrant parenting, particularly among middle-class Chinese in Singapore, predominantly focuses on strategies of balance and 'doing security'. However, pre-2026 literature largely overlooks the more extreme, non-normative cases where families or children actively reject either heritage maintenance or host-society integration. Understanding these 'deviant' cases is crucial for challenging the assumed universality of the 'balance' ideal and revealing the full spectrum of immigrant family adaptation under Singapore's specific bilingual and multicultural policies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cases of outright rejection (of heritage or integration) are not failures of adaptation but are strategic, context-driven responses to perceived threats to class status, child success, or family cohesion, shaped by specific intersections of Singapore's education system, transnational class anxieties, and peer dynamics.
proposals.method: This study employs a qualitative, multi-sited comparative case study design. We will conduct in-depth, semi-structured interviews and ethnographic observations with 20-25 middle-class Chinese immigrant families in Singapore, purposively sampled to identify 'extreme' cases (e.g., families abandoning Mandarin education, children refusing cultural practices). Data analysis will utilize a modified constructivist grounded theory approach, employing iterative coding cycles (open, axial, selective) using NVivo software to build theoretical models from the data, complemented by discourse analysis of parental narratives to uncover underlying anxieties and strategic reasoning.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a critical counter-narrative to the dominant 'balance' paradigm in immigrant family studies. It will empirically map the conditions and consequences of polarized adaptation strategies, offering new theoretical insights into how transnational class projects can manifest as cultural rejection. The findings will provide nuanced evidence for Singaporean policymakers on the limits and pressures of its bilingual and integration framework.
proposals.novelty: While existing work by Yang and others examines the tensions and inconsistencies in pursuing balance, this proposal uniquely investigates the logical endpoints of those tensions—complete rejection—which remain empirically underexplored and theoretically significant for understanding the boundaries of immigrant adaptation strategies.

proposals.title: Strategic De-prioritization: A Study of Middle-Class Chinese Immigrant Families in Singapore Choosing Non-Linguistic Cultural Transmission
proposals.motivation: Research on immigrant families often frames heritage language maintenance as the primary or default strategy for cultural reproduction. However, pre-2026 studies, including Yang's work, reveal the significant challenges parents face within Singapore's bilingual education system and the complex anxieties of transnational class mobility. This proposal investigates the under-explored alternative: families who consciously deprioritize linguistic transmission in favor of other cultural markers, a strategic choice that existing literature often overlooks or pathologizes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a distinct subset of middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore, the strategic de-prioritization of heritage language is a calculated, class-based strategy aimed at securing children's academic and socioeconomic futures, with cultural identity being actively maintained through non-linguistic practices such as values, rituals, and transnational social capital.
proposals.method: This qualitative study will employ a multi-method, longitudinal inquiry over 18 months. We will conduct in-depth, semi-structured interviews and ethnographic observations with 25-30 families identified through purposive and snowball sampling. Data analysis will utilize a grounded theory approach, facilitated by NVivo software, to code for themes of strategy, sacrifice, and alternative cultural markers. This will be complemented by a focused discourse analysis of parents' narratives around 'success' and 'Chineseness' to understand the construction of their parenting logic.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a more nuanced typology of immigrant cultural transmission strategies, moving beyond a deficit model of language loss. It will empirically document and theorize the concept of 'strategic de-prioritization' as a conscious adaptation to host-country education systems and transnational class projects. The findings will challenge assumptions in language policy and immigrant integration literature by highlighting the agency and calculated trade-offs made by middle-class immigrant parents.
proposals.novelty: While existing work documents the struggles of language maintenance, this study uniquely focuses on families who have made a deliberate, active choice to *not* prioritize it, examining this not as a failure but as a coherent strategy. It innovatively shifts the analytical lens from linguistic outcomes to the broader repertoire of cultural practices parents deploy to navigate the specific pressures of Singapore's competitive education landscape and their own transnational class anxieties.

proposals.title: Negotiating Identity in the Intergenerational Gap: A Comparative Study of Second-Generation Chinese Immigrant Children and Their Parents in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on Chinese immigrant families in Singapore, including Dr. Yang's work, has focused intensely on parental anxieties, strategies, and discourses, revealing a complex negotiation of class, culture, and educational systems. However, this creates a significant empirical gap: the perspectives of the children who are the subjects of these intensive parenting projects are largely absent. Understanding the children's own identity formations, linguistic attitudes, and sense of belonging is crucial for a complete picture of immigrant family dynamics and the long-term outcomes of transnational mobility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that second-generation children's self-perceived identities, educational aspirations, and attitudes toward heritage language will differ significantly from, and often exist in creative tension with, the projected anxieties and strategic 'security' frameworks constructed by their middle-class immigrant parents.
proposals.method: This study employs a qualitative, child-centered comparative design. We will conduct semi-structured, in-depth interviews with 20-25 dyads/triads (a child aged 12-16 and at least one parent) from middle-class Chinese immigrant families in Singapore. Interviews will be conducted separately to ensure independent perspectives. Data analysis will use a modified constructivist grounded theory approach, involving iterative coding (open, axial, selective) in NVivo software. The core analytical technique will be a systematic thematic comparison between parent and child transcripts within each family unit, followed by cross-family analysis to identify patterns of convergence, divergence, and negotiation.
proposals.contribution: This research will directly contribute to the sociology of immigrant families and education by providing the missing child's voice, thus moving beyond parent-centric models. It will empirically test the intergenerational transmission of class and cultural capital in a transnational context. The findings will offer nuanced insights for Singapore's education and integration policies, particularly regarding bilingualism and the socio-emotional well-being of second-generation youth.
proposals.novelty: While existing work analyzes parental discourse, this study innovates by systematically capturing and contrasting the child's agentic perspective with the parental framework within the same family, revealing the lived experience of the 'project' of immigrant upbringing. It shifts the analytical focus from parenting strategies to the intergenerational *interface* where identity is co-constructed.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Trajectories of Identity and Aspiration: A Multi-Method Study of Middle-Class Chinese Immigrant Families in Singapore
proposals.motivation: The identity development of children in transnational contexts is a critical but understudied process, shaped by evolving parental anxieties and Singapore's competitive education system. Existing cross-sectional studies, including Yang's prior work, provide snapshots but cannot capture how family strategies and child identities co-evolve over key developmental stages, particularly as children navigate the pressures of bilingualism and academic streaming. A longitudinal design is essential to understand the dynamic interplay between parental 'security projects' and child agency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the identity narratives and educational aspirations of children in middle-class Chinese immigrant families will show significant inflection points corresponding to key Singaporean educational milestones (e.g., PSLE), and that parental strategies will shift from direct cultural transmission to more negotiated forms of influence as children age and assert their own hybrid identities.
proposals.method: This study will employ a longitudinal, multi-method qualitative design, tracking 25-30 families over 5 years with annual data collection waves. We will use repeated, in-depth phenomenological interviews with parents and children (aged 8-13 at baseline), paired with participatory methods like identity maps and photo-elicitation with children. Data analysis will utilize a longitudinal qualitative framework, employing iterative thematic analysis and narrative trajectory analysis to identify patterns of change, continuity, and turning points within and across family cases.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a dynamic model of transnational identity formation, moving beyond static portraits of parenting to show how intergenerational negotiations unfold. It will contribute novel insights to the sociology of education and migration by linking macro-level institutional pressures (Singapore's bilingual system, class structure) to micro-level familial adaptations over time, offering a richer understanding of immigrant integration and belonging.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines immigrant parenting at a single point in time, this study's longitudinal, child-centric design uniquely captures the bidirectional and temporal dynamics of influence, revealing how identities are not simply transmitted but are continually co-constructed and revised within the family.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Societal Impact: A Mixed-Methods Study of Middle-Class Chinese Immigrant Parenting Strategies on Social Cohesion in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Singapore's social stability is predicated on its multicultural policy and social cohesion. Prior qualitative work by Yang and others reveals that middle-class Chinese immigrant parents employ complex strategies navigating education and identity. The impact of these micro-level family strategies on macro-level societal integration remains unmeasured, creating a critical gap in understanding the long-term health of Singapore's multicultural fabric.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, measurable parental strategies—such as investment in mother-tongue education, participation in cross-ethnic community activities, and the framing of educational success—are significant predictors of both perceived and behavioral indicators of social cohesion within their networks and the broader community.
proposals.method: We will employ a sequential mixed-methods design. First, we will conduct in-depth interviews (n=40) to refine constructs. Second, we will develop and validate a survey instrument measuring parental strategies (independent variables) and multi-dimensional cohesion metrics (dependent variables: bridging social capital, multicultural attitudes, institutional trust). Third, we will administer the survey to a stratified sample of 500 middle-class Chinese immigrant parents. Analysis will use structural equation modeling (SEM) to test pathways and quantify effect sizes, complemented by social network analysis of community engagement data.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative framework linking immigrant parenting practices to societal outcomes in Singapore. It will offer policymakers evidence-based metrics to evaluate integration initiatives beyond academic achievement. For academia, it bridges family sociology with social cohesion theory using robust measurement.
proposals.novelty: While existing work descriptively analyzes parenting discourses, this project innovates by developing and validating quantitative metrics to model the causal impact of these practices on social cohesion, moving from narrative to measurement.

proposals.title: Digital Traces of Transnational Childhood: A Network Analysis of Cultural Consumption Among Chinese Immigrant Children in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Research on immigrant parenting, particularly among middle-class Chinese in Singapore, has relied heavily on parent interviews and surveys, which may not capture children's actual cultural engagement. This gap is critical as children navigate Singapore's bilingual system and global digital culture, potentially creating a divergence between parental aspirations and children's lived experiences. Understanding this divergence is key to analyzing intergenerational transmission and identity formation in transnational contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the digital cultural consumption networks of middle-class Chinese immigrant children in Singapore will reveal hybrid and transnational affiliations that are systematically misaligned with the cultural and linguistic priorities reported by their parents, reflecting a distinct, peer-influenced 'third space' of engagement.
proposals.method: We will collect publicly available digital trace data (e.g., follows, likes, shares) from social media platforms popular with youth (e.g., Instagram, TikTok, Douyin) for a purposive sample of children (aged 12-18) from our established parent study cohorts. Using Python libraries (NetworkX, Gephi), we will construct bipartite affiliation networks linking children to cultural content (music, media, influencers). Community detection algorithms (e.g., Louvain method) will map clusters of consumption, which we will qualitatively code for cultural/linguistic markers. These network maps will be triangulated with parent interview data on educational strategies and cultural goals.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical, network-based map of the cultural 'third space' inhabited by immigrant children, moving beyond parental discourse. It will contribute to theories of transnational childhood and intensive parenting by quantifying the gap between parental cultural projects and children's digital practices. Methodologically, it pioneers the integration of digital trace analysis with qualitative immigration studies in education.
proposals.novelty: While prior work analyzes parent *discourse* on culture, this study directly analyzes children's *digital behavior* as a proxy for cultural engagement. Applying computational social science network methods to the specific context of Chinese immigrant families in Singapore is a novel interdisciplinary synthesis.

proposals.title: Fractured Middle-Class Strategies: A Comparative Analysis of Parenting Discourses Among Chinese Immigrants in Singapore by Profession, Migration Pathway, and Economic Capital
proposals.motivation: Existing research on middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore often treats this group as homogeneous, focusing on shared anxieties and 'tiger parenting' tropes. This overlooks significant internal stratification based on factors like professional status, migration pathway (e.g., Employment Pass vs. Permanent Resident), and income stability, which likely shape distinct educational strategies and security-seeking behaviors. A nuanced analysis is crucial for understanding how class is performed and contested within transnational spaces, moving beyond monolithic portrayals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that significant fractures exist within the middle-class Chinese immigrant cohort in Singapore, where parents in high-autonomy professions (e.g., finance, tech), those with precarious employment, and those who migrated as entrepreneurs will articulate fundamentally different parenting discourses, resource mobilization strategies, and engagements with Singapore's bilingual education system compared to those in stable, state-aligned professions (e.g., academia, engineering).
proposals.method: The study will employ a comparative qualitative design using in-depth, semi-structured interviews (n≈45-60) with Chinese immigrant parents, stratified by profession, migration visa history, and self-reported economic security. Data analysis will utilize a modified constructivist grounded theory approach, facilitated by NVivo software. We will employ constant comparative analysis to identify discursive patterns and thematic divergences, specifically using framework analysis to systematically compare cases across the defined stratifying variables, moving beyond thematic description to explain variation.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a more granular, empirically grounded theory of middle-class immigrant parenting that accounts for internal differentiation. It will advance the sociology of education and migration by linking micro-level parenting practices to meso-level structures of profession and migration policy. The findings will also offer practical insights for Singaporean educational institutions in addressing the diverse needs of immigrant families.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Yang and others identifies a shared middle-class immigrant experience, this study's novelty lies in systematically deconstructing that category to reveal the contested and variable nature of 'middle-classness' itself in a transnational context, tying specific parenting discourses directly to professional subcultures and state-controlled migration pathways.

proposals.title: Child-Driven Parenting Pressures: How Children's Academic Demands and Peer Comparisons Shape the Educational Strategies of Middle-Class Chinese Immigrant Parents in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Prior research on middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore, including Yang's work, identifies parental inconsistency and anxiety as key drivers of intensive, controlling educational practices. However, this parent-centric view overlooks the potential agency of children. In Singapore's highly competitive, exam-oriented education system (pre-2026), understanding if and how children actively pressure parents to adopt strategies the parents initially resisted is crucial for a complete sociological model of transnational parenting.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that children of middle-class Chinese immigrant families in Singapore, through their expressed academic struggles, demands for specific resources, and reports of peer competition, exert significant upward pressure on their parents, leading parents to adopt more controlling or 'tiger' parenting practices despite their initial reluctance or ideological rejection of such methods.
proposals.method: This qualitative study will employ a longitudinal, child-inclusive design. We will conduct separate, in-depth semi-structured interviews with 25-30 parent-child dyads (children aged 10-14) at two time points, six months apart. Thematic analysis, informed by constructivist grounded theory, will be used to code interview transcripts. Crucially, we will employ a 'triangulated pressure mapping' technique, comparing parent narratives of their evolving strategies with child narratives of their requests, complaints, and peer observations to identify causal links and dissonances.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a novel, child-agentic dimension to the literature on immigrant parenting and educational strategizing. It will challenge the dominant framework of parenting as a top-down process by empirically demonstrating how children are active co-constructors of family educational climates. The findings will provide a more nuanced understanding of the 'inconsistency' observed in parental discourse by tracing its origins to dynamic intra-family negotiations.
proposals.novelty: While existing work frames inconsistency as arising from parental internal conflict or socio-structural pressures, this study innovates by inverting the causal lens to investigate children as a direct source of that inconsistency, a perspective largely absent in pre-2026 studies of Asian immigrant families.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Trajectories of Parenting: A Multi-Method Study of Discourse-Practice Dynamics Among Middle-Class Chinese Immigrant Families in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Prior research by Yang and others has identified a significant discourse-practice gap among middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore, revealing tensions between stated parenting ideologies and enacted strategies. However, these studies are cross-sectional, offering only a snapshot. It remains unknown how these gaps evolve as children age and families acculturate, a critical question for understanding the long-term adaptation of immigrant families and the effectiveness of Singapore's bilingual and educational policies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the discourse-practice gap will widen over time as children progress through Singapore's high-stakes education system, with parents' practices becoming increasingly instrumental and security-driven, even as their discursive justifications may shift towards more child-centric or localized narratives.
proposals.method: This longitudinal, mixed-methods study will track a cohort of 30-40 middle-class Chinese immigrant families over three years. We will employ annual, in-depth phenomenological interviews with parents, repeated participant observation of key educational routines (e.g., homework, enrichment classes), and digital diary methods. Data analysis will use a combination of longitudinal qualitative analysis (LQA) to track narrative shifts and repeated-measures thematic analysis to code and quantify changes in reported practices versus stated beliefs, enabling a detailed mapping of evolving discrepancies.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal account of parenting evolution in this context, moving beyond static 'gaps' to model dynamic trajectories. It will contribute empirically to theories of transnational parenting and acculturation, and offer policy-relevant insights into how family strategies interact with Singapore's education system over the critical primary school years.
proposals.novelty: While existing work identifies the gap, this project's longitudinal design is novel, allowing it to capture *how* and *why* parenting discourses and practices co-evolve under systemic pressure, testing whether initial inconsistencies become entrenched patterns or resolve over time.

proposals.title: Negotiating the Gap: Children's Interpretations of Contradictory Parenting Discourses and Practices in Middle-Class Chinese Immigrant Families in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Prior research on middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore has identified a significant discourse-practice gap, where parents express ambivalence about 'tiger parenting' yet often engage in intensive educational strategies. This creates a complex family environment, but the children's experiences and interpretations of these contradictions remain unexplored. Understanding the child's perspective is crucial for assessing the psychosocial impacts of transnational mobility and educational pressure in a high-stakes context like Singapore.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that children actively perceive and interpret the gap between their parents' expressed parenting ideals and their actual practices, developing nuanced coping and negotiation strategies that are shaped by their own bilingual/bicultural positioning within Singapore's education system.
proposals.method: This qualitative study will conduct semi-structured, in-depth interviews with 25-30 children (aged 12-16) from the families studied in prior work. Interviews will use child-centered techniques like vignettes and scenario discussions to elicit perceptions of parental expectations, pressures, and inconsistencies. Data will be analyzed using reflexive thematic analysis, with a specific focus on applying an 'interpretive reproduction' framework to understand how children actively make sense of and participate in family educational projects.
proposals.contribution: This study will contribute a critical child-centered dimension to the literature on immigrant parenting and educational stratification, moving beyond parental accounts. It will generate new insights into how intergenerational dynamics and children's agency shape the outcomes of transnational class strategies. Findings will inform educational and family support services for immigrant communities in competitive academic environments.
proposals.novelty: While existing work documents parental anxieties and inconsistencies, this project is novel in directly investigating the children's perspectives on this gap, treating them as active interpreters rather than passive recipients of parenting. It uniquely applies the lens of 'interpretive reproduction' to the context of middle-class immigrant educational strategies.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Discourse-Practice Gap: A Large-Scale Survey of Parenting Ideologies and Educational Strategies Among Chinese Immigrant Families in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Prior qualitative studies by Yang and others have revealed a complex 'discourse-practice gap' among middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore, where expressed parenting ideologies often conflict with enacted strategies. However, the prevalence, demographic correlates, and systemic drivers of this gap across the broader immigrant population remain unknown. A quantitative assessment is crucial for informing targeted educational and social policies that address the real, rather than perceived, challenges of immigrant integration and family support in Singapore's competitive landscape.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the discourse-practice gap in parenting is a widespread phenomenon among Chinese immigrant families in Singapore, and that its magnitude is systematically predicted by factors such as length of residency, socio-economic status, perceived institutional barriers, and levels of 'transnational class anxiety'.
proposals.method: We will design and administer a structured survey to a stratified random sample of 800-1000 first-generation Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore. The instrument will operationalize key constructs from prior qualitative work (e.g., 'tiger parenting' discourse, 'security-seeking' practices, mother-tongue attitudes) into validated Likert-scale and behavioral frequency items. We will use confirmatory factor analysis to validate scales, followed by multiple regression and latent class analysis to test predictors of the discourse-practice gap and identify distinct sub-populations within the immigrant community.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first population-level estimates of the parenting discourse-practice gap, moving beyond rich, contextual case studies to generalizable patterns. It will identify key demographic and psychological levers of parental behavior, offering evidence for policymakers in education and immigrant services. The validated survey instrument will also enable future longitudinal and comparative research on immigrant family adaptation.
proposals.novelty: While the gap itself has been qualitatively described, this study innovates by developing quantitative measures for these nuanced sociocultural constructs and testing their distribution and predictors at scale, bridging interpretive sociology with demographic and social psychology methods in migration studies.

proposals.title: Transnational Contexts of Anxiety: A Comparative Study of Middle-Class Chinese Immigrant Parenting in Singapore, Hong Kong, and Sydney
proposals.motivation: Prior research on middle-class Chinese immigrant parents has focused intensely on Singapore, revealing complex negotiations of class, security, and educational pressure. However, it remains unclear how these parenting strategies and anxieties are shaped by specific host-society institutions and competitive landscapes. A systematic comparison with other major immigrant hubs is crucial to disentangle the effects of transnational mobility from local contextual pressures, advancing theories of global middle-class formation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that while middle-class Chinese immigrant parents across global hubs share a transnational orientation towards educational security, their specific parenting discourses and practices will vary significantly based on the perceived structure of opportunity and the institutional 'rules of the game' in each host society's education system.
proposals.method: This study employs a comparative qualitative design, extending Yang's established methodological approach. We will conduct 45-60 in-depth, semi-structured interviews with middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore, Hong Kong, and Sydney (15-20 per site), matched by socioeconomic and migration trajectory. Data analysis will utilize a multi-stage thematic coding process, facilitated by NVivo software. Following initial open coding, we will employ a framework analysis to systematically compare themes across sites, focusing on discursive strategies, perceived institutional constraints, and enacted practices, ensuring analytical rigor through constant comparative methods and peer debriefing.
proposals.contribution: This research will generate a refined, context-sensitive model of transnational middle-class parenting, moving beyond a single-site analysis. It will contribute empirically to the sociology of education and migration by mapping how global anxieties are locally mediated. The findings will offer policymakers in competitive education systems insights into the experiences of a key demographic.
proposals.novelty: While existing work details the Singaporean case, this study is novel in its deliberate, controlled cross-context comparison, treating the host society's institutional environment as a key variable rather than a backdrop. It directly tests the portability of concepts like 'transnational class anxiety' and 'reluctant tiger parenting' across distinct policy regimes.

proposals.title: Beyond Utility: A Qualitative Inquiry into Intrinsic and Identity-Based Motivations for Mother Tongue Language Education among Chinese Immigrant Parents in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Prior research on immigrant parents' educational strategies, including Yang's work, predominantly frames Mother Tongue Language (MTL) choice within instrumental logics of academic advantage, social mobility, and transnational class security. This overlooks a significant segment of parents whose decisions are driven by non-instrumental values, such as cultural identity preservation, emotional connection, or intergenerational belonging. Understanding these intrinsic motivations is crucial for a holistic view of immigrant family dynamics and for informing Singapore's bilingual education policy beyond its utilitarian foundations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a distinct, under-studied cohort of middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore prioritizes MTL education primarily for intrinsic, identity-based reasons, and that their parenting narratives and practices differ systematically from those of parents motivated by academic-instrumental concerns.
proposals.method: This study employs a qualitative, comparative case study design. We will conduct in-depth, semi-structured interviews with 30-40 Chinese immigrant parents, purposively sampled to capture variation in MTL motivation (instrumental vs. intrinsic/identity-based). Data analysis will follow a two-stage thematic analysis approach: first, using inductive coding to identify emergent themes around values and decision-making; second, applying a framework of 'parenting capital' (Lareau) and 'emotional transnationalism' (pre-2026 concepts) to perform a cross-case comparative analysis, examining differences in discourse, practice, and engagement with Singapore's education system.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a more nuanced typology of immigrant parental motivations, challenging the dominance of 'Tiger parent' and hyper-instrumental narratives in the literature. It will empirically map the role of intrinsic cultural and identity values in educational decision-making, offering new insights for theories of transnational parenting and for Singapore's policy discussions on sustaining bilingualism in a diverse immigrant society.
proposals.novelty: While existing work documents the pervasive instrumentalism in immigrant parenting, this study deliberately seeks out and centers the counter-narrative of intrinsic motivation, a perspective largely marginalized in prior scholarship on East Asian immigrant families in high-pressure academic environments like Singapore.

proposals.title: Navigating the 'Third Language' Dilemma: Mother Tongue Policy, Identity, and Educational Strategy Among Non-Chinese Immigrant Parents in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Singapore's bilingual education policy, with its focus on English and an official 'Mother Tongue' (MTL), presents distinct challenges for non-Chinese immigrant families (e.g., Indian, Filipino, Western) whose home languages (e.g., Tamil, Hindi, Tagalog, Spanish) are not officially recognized as MTLs for their children. Pre-2026 research on immigrant parenting in Singapore has focused predominantly on Chinese immigrants, creating a significant gap in understanding how other groups navigate this institutional mismatch, which impacts cultural identity transmission, academic strategy, and a sense of belonging.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that non-Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore employ a tripartite strategic framework—encompassing compliance, supplementation, and negotiation—to manage the dissonance between state-mandated MTL options (e.g., taking Malay or a non-heritage Chinese) and their heritage language goals, and that these strategies are shaped by transnational class resources and perceptions of Singapore's multicultural hierarchy.
proposals.method: This study will employ a comparative, multi-method qualitative design. We will conduct in-depth, semi-structured interviews with 30-40 middle-class parents from Indian (non-Tamil speaking), Filipino, and Western expatriate backgrounds. Following constructivist grounded theory principles, iterative coding (using NVivo software) will identify emergent themes around language ideology, educational choice, and identity work. This will be supplemented by discourse analysis of parenting forum discussions and a policy document review to contextualize parental narratives within Singapore's socio-educational landscape.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a critical comparative lens to the literature on immigrant parenting and education policy, moving beyond the Sino-centric focus. It will theorize the 'third language dilemma' as a specific form of institutional exclusion, detailing the strategic agency of underrepresented immigrant groups. The findings will offer evidence-based insights for Singaporean policymakers considering more inclusive adaptations of the bilingual model.
proposals.novelty: While existing work details Chinese immigrants' navigation of a familiar MTL (Chinese), this study uniquely investigates the experiences of groups for whom all official MTL choices represent a non-heritage 'third language,' revealing novel dynamics of compromise, cultural prioritization, and strategic resistance within a rigid bilingual system.

proposals.title: Quantifying the 'Parenting Portfolio': A Mixed-Methods Analysis of Parental Investment Strategies and Mother Tongue Language Proficiency among Middle-Class Chinese Immigrant Children in Singapore
proposals.motivation: For middle-class Chinese immigrant families in Singapore, navigating the bilingual education policy presents a significant challenge, with children's Mother Tongue Language (MTL) proficiency being a key site of parental anxiety and investment. Existing qualitative studies, including Yang's prior work, have documented the diverse strategies parents employ, from private tuition to home-based cultural activities, but the relative effectiveness of these discrete investments remains empirically unmeasured. This gap hinders evidence-based guidance for families and policymakers aiming to support bilingual outcomes within Singapore's unique socio-educational context.
