proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that parental investment in structured, school-aligned strategies (e.g., formal MTL tuition) will show a stronger positive correlation with children's standardized MTL academic scores, whereas investment in informal, culturally-embedded home activities (e.g., heritage media consumption, family conversations) will show a stronger correlation with children's affective engagement and self-reported proficiency in MTL.
proposals.method: This study employs a sequential explanatory mixed-methods design. First, a structured survey will be administered to approximately 300 middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore, quantifying time and financial investments across strategy types (tuition, home activities, etc.). These data will be linked to children's school-based MTL scores and a child self-report scale on MTL engagement. Statistical analysis will use multiple regression and structural equation modeling (SEM) to model the relative impact of each strategy cluster. Subsequently, purposive sampling will select 30-40 survey participants for in-depth, semi-structured interviews, using a comparative case study approach to explore the mechanisms behind the quantitative findings.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first quantitative model mapping specific parental investments to distinct MTL proficiency outcomes, moving beyond descriptive accounts of parenting discourse. It will contribute to sociological theories of 'concerted cultivation' in transnational contexts by testing its efficacy for a non-dominant cultural capital. The findings will offer concrete, evidence-based insights for Singapore's education stakeholders on how to better support immigrant families in achieving bilingual goals.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Yang and others has richly described the *discourses* and *anxieties* of these parents, this study innovates by applying a rigorous, mixed-methods 'portfolio analysis' to empirically *measure the returns* on different parental investments. It uniquely disaggregates 'parental support' into discrete, measurable components to test their efficacy, a novel approach in the study of immigrant education in Singapore.

proposals.title: Ecological Momentary Assessment of Affective and Strategic Dimensions in Mother Tongue Language Homework Support: A Study of Chinese Immigrant Parents in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Prior research on middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore reveals significant tensions in their engagement with Singapore's bilingual education policy, particularly regarding Mother Tongue Language (MTL) homework support. These parents navigate transnational class anxieties and inconsistent parenting discourses, but existing studies rely on retrospective interviews and surveys, which are subject to recall bias and fail to capture the real-time, in-situ dynamics of parental decision-making and emotional labor. Understanding these immediate, contextual challenges is crucial for developing effective educational support structures for immigrant families.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the use of Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) will reveal significant, context-dependent fluctuations in parental affect (e.g., stress, efficacy) and strategic adaptation during MTL homework support, which are systematically linked to specific child behaviors, time pressures, and parents' own linguistic capital, and that these micro-processes underlie the broader 'inconsistent' and 'reluctant' parenting patterns identified in prior qualitative work.
proposals.method: We will employ a smartphone-based Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) protocol over a 14-day period with 50-60 middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore. Using a dedicated app, participants will receive 4-5 random prompts per day during typical homework hours, querying immediate context (location, time pressure), child engagement, parental affect (via a 5-point scale for stress, confidence, frustration), and support strategies used. Event-contingent recordings will be triggered by parents at the start and end of homework sessions. Data analysis will involve multilevel modeling to examine within-person and between-person predictors of affect and strategy choice, and qualitative analysis of open-ended responses to identify emergent in-the-moment challenges.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first fine-grained, real-time dataset on the micro-dynamics of immigrant parental educational labor, moving beyond broad discourses to quantify affective and behavioral variability. It will contribute empirically to theories of intensive parenting and transnational habitus by linking macro-social pressures to micro-interactional data. The findings will offer concrete evidence for educators and policymakers to design timely, context-sensitive interventions for family language support.
proposals.novelty: While EMA is established in psychology, its application to the study of immigrant parenting and educational practice is novel, offering an unprecedented methodological lens to capture the lived, momentary experience behind well-documented sociological patterns. This bridges a gap between macro-level discourse analysis and the micro-foundations of daily practice.

proposals.title: Networked Navigation: A Social Network Analysis of Resource and Information Flows Among Chinese Immigrant Parents in Singapore's Bilingual Education System
proposals.motivation: Singapore's bilingual education policy presents a significant challenge for immigrant families, particularly regarding Mother Tongue Language (MTL) education, as shown in Yang's prior work. The strategies parents use to navigate this system are not developed in isolation but are shaped by their embeddedness in social and digital communities. Understanding the structure and function of these parental support networks is crucial for comprehending how educational inequalities are reproduced or mitigated within transnational contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the structure of information-sharing networks among middle-class Chinese immigrant parents is stratified, creating core-periphery dynamics where well-connected 'broker' parents control access to critical, tacit knowledge about the bilingual system, thereby influencing educational strategies and outcomes for children.
proposals.method: The study will employ a mixed-methods social network analysis (SNA). Data will be collected via name-generator surveys and digital ethnography of key online forums (e.g., Facebook/WeChat groups). Network metrics (e.g., density, centrality, betweenness, modularity for community detection) will be calculated using Python libraries (NetworkX) to map resource flows. This quantitative mapping will be integrated with qualitative interviews focused on network alters identified as high-centrality nodes or structural holes to understand the nature of shared resources and strategic advice.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first empirical map of the support networks Chinese immigrant parents utilize, moving beyond individual parenting discourses to a structural analysis of collective navigation. It will contribute to theories of transnational cultural capital by showing how it is circulated and brokered within community networks, and offer concrete insights for educational institutions seeking to engage more effectively with immigrant families.
proposals.novelty: While existing work examines parental discourse and anxiety, this study innovatively applies SNA—a method common in sociology but less so in education migration studies—to objectively trace and analyze the relational infrastructure that underpins and shapes those very discourses and strategic actions.

proposals.title: The Micro-Politics of Mother Tongue: A Year-Long Micro-Ethnography of Language Socialization in a Chinese Immigrant Family in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Prior research on middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore has identified broad patterns of 'transnational class anxieties' and inconsistent parenting discourses regarding education and bilingualism. However, these studies, often based on interviews and surveys, lack the granular, longitudinal data needed to understand how language ideologies and pragmatic decisions are negotiated in the intimate, daily interactions of family life. This gap is critical as Singapore's bilingual policy and global mobility make the home a primary, contested site for heritage language transmission.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the family's daily Mother Tongue Language (MTL) practices are not a simple enactment of stated beliefs or policy, but a dynamic, micro-political process where parental strategies are continuously adapted in response to children's agency, school pressures, and shifting transnational aspirations, revealing a more complex and contingent model of 'doing security' than previously documented.
proposals.method: This study employs a single-case, longitudinal micro-ethnography over 12 months. The technical approach involves: 1) Participant observation in the home, focusing on naturally occurring MTL-related interactions (e.g., homework help, leisure reading, digital media use). 2) Video/audio recording of selected weekly routines and 'critical incidents'. 3) Bi-weekly reflective interviews with parents and (age-appropriate) children to elicit narratives on recent practices. 4) Analysis using iterative, grounded theory coding (Charmaz, 2014) and micro-analysis of interaction (Erickson, 1992) to trace decision-making trajectories and identify the emergent, situated logic of their MTL engagement.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a uniquely thick, processual account of immigrant family language policy-in-practice, moving beyond snapshot accounts of parental attitudes. It will generate a fine-grained theoretical model of how macro-level forces (policy, transnationalism) are mediated through micro-interactions. The intensive case study will also provide a methodological template for scaling down in migration and family studies to capture lived complexity.
proposals.novelty: While existing work documents *what* parents think or report doing, this micro-ethnography captures *how* and *why* MTL practices unfold and change in real time within one family system, centering child agency and intra-family negotiation as key drivers—a perspective largely absent in the pre-2026 literature on this population.

proposals.title: Beyond Class: Religious and Ethnic Identity Preservation as Competing Drivers of Parenting Anxieties Among Chinese Immigrant Parents in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore, including Yang's work, predominantly frames their anxieties and intensive parenting practices through the lens of class maintenance and transnational mobility. However, Singapore's multicultural, multi-religious society and its bilingual education policy present distinct pressures for ethnic and cultural identity preservation. This study investigates whether these non-class identity factors may be equally or more significant drivers of parental anxiety, a dimension underexplored in the existing literature.
proposals.hypothesis: For middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore, anxieties related to the preservation of religious identity (e.g., Buddhist, Christian) and/or specific ethnic-cultural heritage (e.g., dialect group traditions) are a primary, and sometimes stronger, driver of parenting strategies and educational choices than anxieties related to class status reproduction.
proposals.method: This qualitative study employs a comparative, multi-sited inquiry. We will conduct in-depth, semi-structured interviews with 40-50 middle-class Chinese immigrant parents, strategically sampled to ensure diversity in religious affiliation (e.g., Buddhist, Christian, non-religious) and regional Chinese origin. Thematic analysis, using NVivo software, will code interview transcripts for discourses of anxiety, aspiration, and strategy. The analysis will specifically contrast the salience and emotional weight of themes related to class/status (e.g., university rankings, professional pathways) against themes of religious/ethnic identity (e.g., moral community, language loss, ritual practice, intermarriage fears).
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a more nuanced theoretical framework for understanding immigrant parenting, challenging the primacy of class analysis. It will contribute empirically to the sociology of education and migration by highlighting how Singapore's specific socio-political context shapes identity projects. The findings could inform more culturally sensitive educational and social policy for immigrant integration.
proposals.novelty: While class is well-established as a key analytic, this proposal systematically pits class anxieties against religious and ethnic identity preservation as competing explanatory drivers within the same population, a direct empirical test not yet conducted in pre-2026 studies of this group. It shifts the focus from 'doing security' for class status to 'doing identity' for cultural continuity.

proposals.title: Beyond Strategic Calculation: Intensive Parenting as Embodied Cultural Habitus Among Middle-Class Chinese Immigrants in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Research on middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore has predominantly framed their intensive parenting as a deliberate strategy for securing class status and future security in a competitive, transnational context. This strategic-instrumental lens, while valuable, risks oversimplifying parenting practices by neglecting the deeply ingrained, pre-reflective cultural dispositions that shape them. A more nuanced understanding is needed to unpack the complex interplay between conscious strategy and unconscious habitus in transnational parenting.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore, intensive parenting is not solely a calculated security strategy but is significantly driven by an embodied cultural habitus—a set of durable, internalized dispositions from their own socialization in China’s educational culture, which operates often below the level of conscious calculation.
proposals.method: This study employs a qualitative, longitudinal inquiry combining in-depth phenomenological interviews and participant observation in family settings over 12-18 months. We will recruit 25-30 parent pairs from previous studies to explore the genesis and enactment of their parenting logics. The analysis will utilize a modified constructivist grounded theory approach, specifically employing Bourdieu’s conceptual tools (habitus, field, capital) as sensitizing concepts. We will code for moments of dissonance, inconsistency, and pre-reflective action to distinguish habitus-driven practices from explicitly strategic ones.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a more layered theoretical model of transnational parenting, bridging sociological theories of habitus with migration and education studies. It will provide empirical evidence on how pre-migration cultural schemas persist and adapt, offering a critical perspective on the limits of rational choice models in understanding family dynamics in migration. The findings will inform policymakers and educators in Singapore about the deep-seated cultural roots of parental behaviors.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in directly challenging and relaxing the dominant assumption of purely strategic rationality in the literature on immigrant 'tiger parenting,' by theorizing and empirically investigating intensity as a culturally embodied habitus. This shifts the explanatory focus from future-oriented calculation to the enduring influence of past socialization.

proposals.title: Trajectories of Security: A Longitudinal Inquiry into the Co-evolution of Children's Educational Outcomes and Parental Anxieties among Middle-Class Chinese Immigrants in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Prior research on middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore, including Yang's work, reveals acute anxieties about social mobility and class reproduction, often expressed through intensive parenting strategies. However, these studies are cross-sectional, capturing a static snapshot. A longitudinal study is crucial to understand how these parental anxieties dynamically respond to their children's evolving educational and early career trajectories, a process central to the immigrant family experience in a competitive, global city-state.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that parental anxieties do not linearly decrease with children's academic success; instead, they transform and recalibrate in response to key educational transitions (e.g., PSLE, O-Levels, university admission), with perceived career prestige and stability of the child's eventual path becoming a more significant determinant of parental 'security' in later stages.
proposals.method: This study employs a mixed-methods longitudinal panel design. We will recruit 40-50 families from Yang's prior studies, conducting annual in-depth, semi-structured interviews with parents over 5 years, coupled with structured surveys measuring anxiety scales (e.g., adapted versions of the Parental Anxiety Scale) and tracking children's objective outcomes. Data analysis will use a combination of thematic analysis for interview transcripts and latent growth curve modeling (LGCM) to statistically model the trajectories of anxiety and their covariance with milestone achievements.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first longitudinal map of how transnational parental anxieties evolve, moving beyond the 'tiger parent' stereotype to show the nuanced, temporal relationship between child outcomes and parental well-being. It will contribute empirically to theories of immigrant adaptation and sociologically to understanding how class anxieties are intergenerationally managed over time within a specific ethno-national group in a global city.
proposals.novelty: While existing work identifies the presence of anxiety, this study's novelty lies in its longitudinal focus on the *process* of anxiety transformation, directly linking the temporal dimension of children's institutional pathways to shifts in parental emotional and strategic dispositions.

proposals.title: Negotiating Security, Performing Fatherhood: The Emotional Labor and Strategic Practices of Middle-Class Chinese Immigrant Fathers in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Research on intensive parenting and 'doing security' among middle-class Chinese immigrants in Singapore has predominantly focused on mothers, rendering fathers' roles and contributions analytically invisible. This gap is significant given the distinct gendered expectations and pressures immigrant fathers face in navigating Singapore's competitive education landscape and their own transnational class anxieties. Understanding their specific emotional labor is crucial for a complete picture of immigrant family adaptation and the reproduction of class in a global city.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that middle-class Chinese immigrant fathers in Singapore engage in a distinct, often understated, form of strategic emotional labor—characterized by practices of economic provisioning, institutional brokering, and narrative reassurance—that is central to the family project of 'doing security' but is obscured by dominant discourses of maternal intensive parenting.
proposals.method: This qualitative study will employ a multi-method, longitudinal inquiry. We will conduct in-depth, semi-structured interviews with 25-30 middle-class Chinese immigrant father-mother dyads, supplemented by ethnographic observations in school-related settings (e.g., parent-teacher meetings, school gate interactions). Data analysis will utilize a constructivist grounded theory approach, involving iterative coding (open, axial, selective) and constant comparative analysis to develop themes around fathers' strategic practices and emotional management, explicitly contrasting them with maternal strategies identified in prior work.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a gendered analysis to the literature on transnational parenting and class reproduction, explicitly theorizing the role of fathers. It will expand the conceptual framework of 'doing security' by identifying its paternal dimensions. Findings will inform policy and community support services in Singapore by highlighting the specific challenges and support needs of immigrant fathers.
proposals.novelty: While existing work documents Chinese immigrant mothers' intensive parenting, this study uniquely shifts the analytical lens to fathers, investigating their non-stereotypical, emotionally-laden strategies. It moves beyond the 'tiger parent' or 'reluctant parent' binary to explore the nuanced, often inconsistent, performance of fatherhood within the specific socio-educational pressures of Singapore.

proposals.title: Quantifying Transnational Parenting Anxieties: A Computational Discourse Analysis of Chinese Immigrant Parent Forums in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Prior qualitative work by Yang and others reveals that middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore navigate complex, often inconsistent, parenting ideologies under transnational pressures. However, the scale, prevalence, and co-occurrence of specific anxieties and adaptive strategies within these communities remain unquantified. Analyzing large-scale, naturally occurring online discourse can systematically map the collective consciousness of this demographic, moving beyond interview-based snapshots.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that computational text analysis of parenting forums will reveal distinct, quantifiable clusters of discourse—centered on academic competition, cultural identity, and systemic navigation—that correlate with specific phases of a child's educational trajectory and the parents' duration of stay in Singapore.
proposals.method: We will collect multi-year text data from prominent Singapore-based Chinese-language online forums (e.g., SingaporeMotherhood, hardwarezone) focusing on parenting sub-forums. Using a BERT-based model (e.g., `bert-base-chinese`) fine-tuned for topic classification and sentiment analysis, we will perform: 1) unsupervised topic modeling (BERTopic or GuidedLDA) to identify prevalent themes; 2) supervised classification to track mentions of key pre-identified constructs (e.g., 'tuition', 'mother tongue', 'PSLE', 'China vs Singapore'); and 3) temporal analysis to correlate discourse shifts with Singapore's academic calendar and policy announcements.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, data-driven map of the preoccupations and strategic exchanges within a transnational parenting community. It will quantify the relative weight of different anxieties (academic, cultural, logistical) and reveal how online platforms serve as key sites for informal knowledge transfer and identity negotiation among immigrant parents, complementing existing ethnographic findings.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses interview data to theorize parental anxieties, this project applies state-of-the-art NLP (pre-2026) to analyze the community's own unfiltered digital discourse at scale, allowing for the discovery of latent discursive patterns and their evolution over time not easily accessible through traditional methods.

proposals.title: Shadowing the 'Doing' of Security: An Ethnographic Study of the Embodied Routines and Micro-Practices of Middle-Class Chinese Immigrant Parenting in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Prior research on middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore, including Yang's work, has effectively documented their anxieties and discursive strategies through interviews and surveys. However, a significant gap exists in understanding how these articulated beliefs and class-based insecurities are enacted, negotiated, and sometimes contradicted in the minute, embodied practices of daily family life. This micro-level, real-time perspective is crucial for moving beyond stated ideologies to grasp the lived, practical sociology of 'doing security' and navigating Singapore's competitive education landscape.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ethnographic shadowing will reveal a significant 'practice-discourse gap,' where parents' articulated 'reluctant tiger parenting' discourse is consistently mediated and reshaped in situ by logistical pressures, children's agency, and the unspoken emotional labor of maintaining transnational class identity, resulting in parenting that is more improvisational and context-dependent than interview accounts suggest.
proposals.method: This project will employ intensive ethnographic shadowing, a methodologically rigorous form of participant observation. We will shadow 12-15 purposefully selected middle-class Chinese immigrant families in Singapore across multiple weekday and weekend routines (e.g., morning preparations, school commutes, tuition/co-curricular sessions, homework, weekend family time). Data collection will involve detailed field notes using a structured template focusing on temporal organization, spatial navigation, verbal/non-verbal interactions, and material artifacts. This will be supplemented by periodic 'headnote' interviews to capture immediate participant reflections. Analysis will follow an iterative, thematic approach, coding for patterns of practice, moments of tension/improvisation, and the intersection of routines with broader structural forces like education policy and migration status.
proposals.contribution: This study will contribute a novel, practice-oriented dimension to the literature on immigrant parenting and educational strategies, providing rich, contextual data on the 'how' of cultural reproduction. It will refine theoretical concepts like 'intensive parenting' and 'transnational capital' by grounding them in observable micro-practices. For policy, it offers insights into the lived experience of families within Singapore's bilingual and streaming systems, beyond official policy evaluations.
proposals.novelty: While interview-based studies capture post-hoc rationalizations, this proposal's novelty lies in its systematic application of shadowing to capture the real-time, often tacit, performance of parenting. This moves the analysis from reported discourse to observed praxis, uncovering the dynamic and sometimes inconsistent 'in-the-moment' decision-making that shapes childrearing in a high-stakes immigrant context.

proposals.title: Transnational Security Strategies in a Mobile World: A Comparative Survey of Middle-Class Chinese Immigrant Parenting Across Global Cities
proposals.motivation: Prior research on middle-class Chinese immigrant parenting, including Yang's work in Singapore, reveals complex strategies of 'doing security' to navigate educational systems and class anxieties. However, these findings are context-specific to single host countries. In a pre-2026 era of heightened global mobility and geopolitical uncertainty, a systematic, multi-country comparison is needed to understand how host-society institutions and perceived global hierarchies shape transnational parenting logics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that middle-class Chinese immigrant parents' security strategies—encompassing educational investments, language policies, and cultural transmission—vary systematically not just by individual capital, but by the perceived permeability of the host country's elite pathways and its position within a global hierarchy of desirable destinations.
proposals.method: We will conduct a large-N, standardized online survey of first-generation Chinese immigrant parents in five global-city hubs: Singapore, Vancouver, London, Sydney, and San Francisco. The survey instrument, developed from prior qualitative findings, will measure dependent variables (educational strategy, mother-tongue effort, extracurricular investment) and key independent variables (perceptions of host-country mobility, transnational network density, and 'global class' anxiety). We will employ multilevel regression modeling to account for individual and city/country-level effects, using structural equation modeling (SEM) to test the pathways between macro-context, parental perception, and strategic action.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative, comparative map of Chinese diaspora parenting strategies, moving beyond single-site ethnography. It will theorize how global city status and national education regimes interact to produce distinct patterns of immigrant family security projects, contributing to broader sociological debates on transnational class formation and the globalization of parenting cultures.
proposals.novelty: While existing work examines Chinese immigrant parenting within one national context, this project's novelty lies in its explicitly comparative design across multiple host societies, allowing for the isolation of host-country institutional effects from more universal ethnic or class dispositions, a gap in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Negotiating Security: Children's Agency and Resistance in the Transnational Upbringing Strategies of Chinese Immigrant Families in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Prior research on middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore has detailed their 'security-driven' parenting, focused on academic and cultural capital accumulation in a competitive, transnational context. However, this literature predominantly examines parental perspectives, rendering children as passive recipients. Understanding children's active negotiation or rejection of these plans is crucial for a complete sociological picture of family dynamics, intergenerational transmission, and the lived experience of immigrant adaptation in Singapore's intense educational landscape.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that children of middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore employ a spectrum of agentic strategies—from covert resistance to overt negotiation—that systematically reshape, delay, or subvert parental security-driven projects, and that these strategies are influenced by the child's age, gender, and degree of immersion in local peer cultures.
proposals.method: This qualitative study will employ a longitudinal, multi-method design. We will conduct repeat, in-depth interviews over 18 months with 25-30 child-parent dyads (children aged 10-16). Child-focused methods will include participant-created visual timelines of weekly activities and semi-structured interviews using vignettes to elicit responses on hypothetical parenting scenarios. Parent interviews will explore their perceptions of child compliance and challenges. Data analysis will utilize a modified grounded theory approach, employing iterative coding in NVivo software to identify themes of resistance, negotiation tactics, and discursive justifications, followed by cross-case analysis to establish patterns based on demographic variables.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a critical child-centric perspective to the literature on transnational parenting and immigrant adaptation, moving beyond a deficit model of child development. It will generate a novel typology of children's agentic responses to intensive, security-oriented upbringing, offering insights for educators and family counselors working with immigrant communities in Singapore.
proposals.novelty: While existing work documents parental anxieties and strategies, this study uniquely positions the child as an agentic actor in the co-construction of family educational projects, explicitly investigating the understudied 'dual problem' of resistance within the specific socio-cultural frame of Chinese-Singaporean transnationalism.

proposals.title: Institutional Amplification of Insecurity: How Singapore's Private Education Industry Markets to Chinese Immigrant Parents
proposals.motivation: Prior research on middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore details their 'security' anxieties and intensive parenting practices, but remains focused on family-level dynamics. The role of the burgeoning private tuition and enrichment industry in shaping, catering to, and amplifying these anxieties is underexplored. An institutional analysis is crucial to understand how market forces systematically transform parental concerns into a commodified educational landscape.
proposals.hypothesis: Private educational institutions (tuition centers, enrichment agencies, international school consultants) do not merely respond to parental demand but actively construct and amplify narratives of academic and social insecurity through targeted marketing, thereby reinforcing and legitimizing the intensive parenting ethos among Chinese immigrant families.
proposals.method: This multi-method study combines critical discourse analysis of marketing materials (websites, brochures, social media) from 30-40 leading private education providers with semi-structured interviews of 20-25 industry managers and consultants. A third component involves a systematic content analysis of online parent forums (e.g., SingaporeMotherhood) using pre-2026 natural language processing techniques (e.g., topic modeling with Latent Dirichlet Allocation and sentiment analysis via VADER) to trace the circulation of institutional marketing narratives into community discourse.
proposals.contribution: It shifts the analytical scale from family practices to the institutional actors that profit from and shape them, contributing to the sociology of education and migration. It provides a concrete analysis of how 'edu-business' markets insecurity, bridging studies of parenting with political economy. The findings will offer a more systemic understanding of the pressures sustaining intensive parenting in competitive immigrant contexts.
proposals.novelty: While existing work examines parents' internal discourses, this project explicitly investigates the supply-side, commercial institutions that fuel the demand. It innovatively combines institutional ethnography with computational text analysis of community data to map the flow of anxiety-driven marketing.

proposals.title: Questioning the Hierarchy of Question Value: A Comparative Analysis of Question Types in the Citizenship Education of Chinese Immigrant Children in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Singapore's bilingual education policy and multicultural society present unique challenges for immigrant parents navigating citizenship formation. Pre-2026 research on middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore reveals anxieties about class, security, and cultural transmission, often manifesting in specific pedagogical interactions with their children. The educational value of different types of questions (e.g., factual recall vs. critical inquiry) for fostering a sense of civic belonging remains an underexplored dimension of this parenting practice.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that, contrary to educational theories privileging open-ended, critical questions, immigrant parents' strategic use of factual and procedural question types is perceived as equally or more valuable for navigating the specific bureaucratic and socio-cultural pathways to secure citizenship and belonging in Singapore.
proposals.method: This qualitative study employs a multi-method inquiry. We will conduct in-depth, semi-structured interviews and diary studies with 30-40 middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore, focusing on their homework help and discussions about Singaporean society. Using pre-2026 qualitative data analysis software (e.g., NVivo 12), we will code for question types (factual, procedural, analytical, evaluative) and their perceived purposes. Discourse analysis will link these practices to parents' articulated strategies for 'doing security' and ensuring their children's successful integration, as identified in Yang's prior work.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a nuanced understanding of how pedagogical tools are adapted within transnational contexts to meet specific citizenship goals. It will challenge universalist assumptions in education by showing how the 'value' of a question is contingent on the immigrant family's strategic navigation of host-country institutions. The findings will add a micro-interactional layer to the literature on immigrant parenting, class, and education.
proposals.novelty: While existing work documents parental anxieties and discourses, this study uniquely zooms in on the specific mechanism of 'question-asking' as a site of cultural and civic labor. It innovatively applies pedagogical frameworks about question hierarchy to the sociology of immigrant integration, testing their validity in a non-Western, highly structured multicultural context.

proposals.title: Pedagogical Translation in a Transnational Classroom: Teacher Understanding and Application of the Three-Question Taxonomy for Chinese Immigrant Students in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Singapore's Social Studies curriculum, with its emphasis on national identity and citizenship, presents unique challenges for immigrant students and their teachers. The three-question taxonomy (What is happening? Why is it important? How do we know?) is a core pedagogical tool designed to develop critical thinking. Its effective implementation is crucial for engaging a diverse student body, including the growing cohort of middle-class Chinese immigrant children whose parents' educational strategies are shaped by transnational anxieties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that teachers' interpretation and operationalization of the three-question taxonomy are significantly mediated by their awareness of the specific socio-cultural and educational dispositions of middle-class Chinese immigrant families, leading to inconsistent pedagogical adaptations intended to bridge the curriculum's national aims with students' transnational experiences.
proposals.method: This qualitative study employs a comparative case study design within Singapore's secondary schools. We will conduct in-depth, semi-structured interviews with 20-25 Social Studies teachers, coupled with classroom observations and document analysis of lesson plans. Data will be analyzed using thematic analysis, informed by constructivist grounded theory. The analytical framework will integrate concepts from teacher cognition, curriculum implementation, and pre-2026 literature on Chinese immigrant parenting and 'doing security' in Singapore's educational landscape.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a novel 'teacher-facing' perspective to the literature on immigrant education in Singapore, which has predominantly focused on parents. It will generate a fine-grained model of how pedagogical tools are filtered through teachers' perceptions of student background, offering practical insights for teacher professional development in culturally responsive Social Studies instruction within a bilingual, multicultural system.
proposals.novelty: While existing work examines immigrant parents' educational strategies, this study uniquely investigates the complementary role of the teacher as a crucial intermediary who interprets and applies core curricular tools in response to the very student identities and family projects that prior research has documented.

proposals.title: Scaffolding Citizenship: Adapting a Three-Question Taxonomy for Primary School Social Studies in Singapore's Immigrant Context
proposals.motivation: Prior research on middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore reveals significant anxieties about their children's academic and social integration, often manifesting in intensive parenting strategies around language and security. The existing three-question taxonomy (What is? What ought to be? What can be?), used to analyze parental discourse, has proven effective for understanding adult reasoning but remains untested for younger students. Adapting this framework for primary school Social Studies is crucial, as this subject is a primary site for formal citizenship education, where children's nascent understandings of identity, community, and national belonging are shaped, potentially intersecting with or diverging from their parents' transnational anxieties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a developmentally appropriate adaptation of the three-question taxonomy can effectively map the conceptual capacities of primary school students in Singapore, revealing how their reasoning about citizenship and community in Social Studies is influenced by their immigrant family background and the national curriculum's objectives.
proposals.method: This qualitative, classroom-based study will employ a design-based research (DBR) methodology. We will first conduct a document analysis of the Primary 3-5 Social Studies syllabus (pre-2026) to align with curriculum objectives. Then, we will iteratively design and test age-appropriate pedagogical scaffolds (e.g., visual prompts, simplified scenarios, sentence starters) to operationalize the three questions for children. Data collection will involve structured classroom observations and focused group interviews using think-aloud protocols with approximately 30 students from diverse immigrant backgrounds. Thematic analysis, guided by the adapted taxonomy, will be used to code student responses and reasoning patterns.
proposals.contribution: This study will contribute a novel, empirically-tested analytical tool for examining young learners' civic reasoning, bridging the gap between sociology of immigration and primary education research. It will provide concrete insights into how children of immigrants engage with state-led citizenship narratives, offering educators a framework to support inclusive pedagogical practices in multicultural classrooms.
proposals.novelty: While the three-question taxonomy has been applied to adult immigrant parents, its adaptation for child learners in a formal educational setting is novel. This shift in focus from parental discourse to children's own reasoning directly within the curriculum represents a significant scaling down and contextual refinement of the original analytical model.

proposals.title: Disaggregating the 'Model Minority' Student: A Critical Inquiry into the Stratified Experiences of Chinese Immigrant Postgraduate Parents in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 literature on middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore predominantly focuses on the K-12 context, treating them as a monolithic group navigating a uniform 'tiger parenting' script. This overlooks the significant, yet understudied, postgraduate student-parent population, whose experiences are shaped by distinct pressures of academia, precarious funding, and adult integration. A nuanced analysis is crucial to move beyond homogenizing narratives and understand how institutional and disciplinary structures within higher education differentially shape parenting practices and family security strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: The parenting discourses and security-seeking strategies of Chinese immigrant postgraduate parents in Singapore are not uniform but are critically stratified by their disciplinary field (e.g., STEM vs. Humanities/Social Sciences), source of funding (e.g., competitive scholarship vs. self-funding), and family migration status (e.g., accompanying spouse/children vs. separated).
proposals.method: This study employs a qualitative, critical discourse analysis (CDA) framework, building on Yang's established methodological approach. We will conduct in-depth, semi-structured interviews with 40-50 Chinese immigrant postgraduate parents at Singaporean universities, using purposive sampling to ensure variation across the key stratifying variables. Interview transcripts will be analyzed using NVivo software for thematic coding, with a specific focus on discursive constructions of 'success,' 'risk,' and 'parental duty.' The analysis will employ constant comparative methods to identify patterns and divergences linked to disciplinary cultures, economic precarity, and transnational family arrangements.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a novel, disaggregated perspective to the sociology of immigrant families and education in Singapore, revealing the internal class and status fractures within the 'middle-class Chinese immigrant' category. It will extend the literature on intensive parenting and 'doing security' into the higher education domain, showing how academic structures become a key site for the reproduction of social inequality. The findings will provide evidence for more targeted university support policies for international postgraduate students with families.
proposals.novelty: While existing work examines Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore's school system, this study is the first to investigate postgraduate students as parents, explicitly challenging their treatment as a homogeneous group by analyzing the intersecting effects of academic discipline and economic capital within the family migration project.

proposals.title: Pedagogizing Immigration: How Universities Use Clinical Supervision Models to Coach International Student Compliance in Singapore
proposals.motivation: Universities are key intermediaries in global student migration, yet research often treats them as passive policy conduits. Pre-2026 scholarship on immigrant families in Singapore highlights intensive parental strategies to navigate educational and state systems, but overlooks the parallel, institutionalized coaching students receive directly from universities. This gap matters because these formal university practices actively shape students' subjectivities and integration pathways, constituting a critical site where immigration policy is pedagogically enacted.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that universities operationalize immigration and integration requirements through structured pedagogical feedback cycles—adapted from teacher clinical supervision—that train international students in performative compliance, thereby transforming them into self-regulating subjects of the host state.
proposals.method: This qualitative study employs Critical Discourse Analysis (CDA) and institutional ethnography. We will conduct participant observation of university orientation sessions, visa advising workshops, and academic integration programs in Singapore. Semi-structured interviews will be held with international students (n=25-30) and university administrative staff (n=15-20). Data analysis will involve iterative coding to identify recurrent feedback routines, discursive framing of 'good' student conduct, and the translation of bureaucratic rules into teachable, assessable behaviors.
proposals.contribution: This research contributes a novel theoretical lens by framing institutional immigration support as a form of pedagogical coaching with a hidden curriculum. It empirically extends the study of immigrant adaptation beyond the family unit to the institutional actors that directly shape migrant subjectivities. The findings will provide a critical understanding of how educational institutions actively produce compliant diasporic citizens.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in fusing concepts from teacher education (clinical supervision, feedback cycles) with migration studies to analyze university administrative practices, moving beyond the dominant focus on parental strategies in immigrant integration literature. It treats the university not just as a context but as an active pedagogical agent of state integration.

proposals.title: From Crisis to Curriculum: Co-Creation, Gatekeeping, and the Institutional Journey of Student Imaginaries in Singapore's Transnational Education Space
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic triggered a global crisis in higher education, forcing a rapid re-imagining of student mobility, support, and learning futures. In Singapore, a key hub for transnational education and immigrant families, this moment created a surge of student-led proposals for hybrid learning, mental health support, and new mobility pathways. This research investigates what happens to these crisis-inspired student imaginaries as institutions transition to a 'new normal', addressing a critical gap in understanding how grassroots innovation is systematically integrated or filtered out by university policy structures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that student imaginaries for post-crisis education undergo a distinct policy lifecycle, where their integration into formal institutional frameworks is not a linear process of adoption but a contested journey shaped by specific gatekeeping practices, class-inflected advocacy resources, and the strategic alignment of student narratives with pre-existing administrative priorities.
proposals.method: This study employs a longitudinal, multi-sited critical discourse analysis (CDA) and institutional ethnography. We will track specific student proposals (e.g., for blended mobility programs, peer mental health networks) from their origin in student forums and focus groups (Year 1) through formal committee meetings and policy drafts (Year 2). Data includes meeting minutes, policy documents, student submissions, and in-depth interviews with student advocates, immigrant parents, and university administrators. Analysis will use iterative coding to map discourse shifts, actor networks, and critical junctures where ideas are captured, diluted, or rejected.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for understanding the 'policy lifecycle' of student agency in higher education, moving beyond static analyses of student voice. It will empirically reveal the mechanisms of institutional co-option and resistance specific to the transnational, market-sensitive context of Singapore. The findings will offer practical insights for designing more equitable student engagement channels that transcend tokenistic consultation.
proposals.novelty: While existing literature examines either student imaginaries or institutional policy-making in isolation, this study uniquely traces the dynamic interaction between them over time. It innovatively applies a transnational class lens—informed by Yang's prior work on immigrant parenting—to analyze how the cultural and social capital of student advocates influences the trajectory of their proposals within Singapore's competitive education landscape.

proposals.title: Translating Meritocracy: A Comparative Institutional Ethnography of Singapore's University Admissions Policy and the Stratification of Immigrant Families
proposals.motivation: In Singapore's highly competitive education landscape, state policies like university admissions criteria are powerful tools of social stratification. For middle-class Chinese immigrant parents, whose pre-2026 anxieties about class reproduction and cultural capital are well-documented, these policies are not abstract but are actively interpreted and enacted by institutions, directly shaping their children's futures. Understanding how a single policy is differentially translated across elite and non-elite higher education institutions is crucial for revealing the mechanisms that sustain or challenge inequality for immigrant families.
proposals.hypothesis: The translation of a central admissions policy (e.g., related to 'holistic' assessment or mother-tongue requirements) across a public flagship, a regional university, and a polytechnic will follow distinct institutional logics, producing stratified 'textual practices' and 'ruling relations' that systematically advantage students from certain backgrounds while creating specific, class-inflected challenges for Chinese immigrant families at each site.
proposals.method: This is a multi-sited institutional ethnography. Data collection will involve: 1) critical discourse analysis of policy texts and institutional implementation documents; 2) in-depth interviews and participant observation with administrators at three institution types (National University of Singapore, Singapore University of Technology and Design, and a polytechnic) to trace policy interpretation; and 3) narrative interviews with faculty and Chinese immigrant parents/students at each site to map the consequences of these translations. Analysis will use Dorothy Smith's Institutional Ethnography framework to explicate the coordinating 'textual practices' and 'ruling relations' that link state policy to local experience.
proposals.contribution: The study will provide a novel, ground-up explanation of how educational stratification is bureaucratically produced in a transnational context, moving beyond parent-focused analyses. It will contribute to the sociology of education by empirically mapping the 'policy translation chain' and to migration studies by detailing how institutional actors mediate immigrant families' incorporation and class anxieties.
proposals.novelty: While existing work examines immigrant parents' strategies, this study flips the lens to the institutional machinery, using a comparative IE design to show how stratification is actively organized through administrative work. It uniquely connects the macro-policy level with the micro-experiences of a specific migrant group across a stratified higher education system.

proposals.title: Routine Frictions: A Critical Discourse Analysis of Policy-Parent Misalignment in Singapore's Education-Migration Nexus
proposals.motivation: Research on policy-practice gaps in migration and education often focuses on acute crises, like pandemic school closures. This overlooks the chronic, everyday tensions arising from the routine interaction of national policies. In pre-2026 Singapore, the state's bilingual education policy, meritocratic ethos, and immigration framework create a stable yet complex environment where middle-class Chinese immigrant parents must navigate subtle but persistent misalignments between their transnational aspirations and local institutional logics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that middle-class Chinese immigrant parents in Singapore experience and articulate a distinct form of 'routine misalignment,' where the cumulative effect of non-crisis policy frictions—particularly between bilingualism goals, academic streaming pressures, and long-term residency uncertainties—generates a specific pattern of discursive negotiation and strategic adaptation that differs from crisis-driven responses.
proposals.method: This study employs Critical Discourse Analysis (CDA) within a longitudinal, multi-sited ethnographic design. We will conduct 40 in-depth, semi-structured interviews with Chinese immigrant parents of children in primary schools, supplemented by participant observation in school events and parent community forums. Interview transcripts and field notes will be analyzed using Fairclough's three-dimensional framework, with coding facilitated by NVivo software to systematically identify discursive strategies (e.g., legitimization, resignation, resistance) used to manage policy tensions. This qualitative inquiry will be contextualized by policy document analysis of Singapore's education and immigration white papers (pre-2026).
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a novel conceptual framework of 'routine policy misalignment' to the sociology of education and migration. It will empirically detail how stable policy environments still produce significant parental anxiety and strategic action, moving beyond crisis-centric models. The findings will offer concrete insights for Singaporean policymakers on the lived experiences of a key demographic within its bilingual and immigration system.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in shifting the analytical focus from dramatic policy failures during emergencies to the subtle, quotidian frictions inherent in a well-regarded, stable policy regime. It applies the misalignment lens to a non-crisis, high-stakes context, revealing how even successful policies can generate consistent sites of tension and negotiation for transnational families.

ideas.title: Structured Institutional Coaching: How Universities Use Pedagogical Feedback Cycles to Mediate International Student Integration
ideas.description: This research investigates how universities, as migration intermediaries, employ formalized coaching and feedback mechanisms borrowed from teacher education to actively shape international students' encounters with immigration policy. It examines structured institutional routines—such as orientation workshops, visa compliance check-ins, and academic advising sessions—as pedagogical feedback cycles that train students to perform specific compliance behaviors. The study analyzes how these coaching practices transfer the logic of clinical supervision from the classroom to the bureaucratic realm, thereby producing students as self-governing subjects of the state. This reveals a hidden curriculum of immigration acculturation, where universities do not just inform policy but pedagogically enact it through daily institutional practices.

ideas.title: The Policy Lifecycle of Student Imaginaries: From Crisis-Inspired Futures to Institutional Co-Creation
ideas.description: This research investigates the lifecycle of crisis-induced student imaginaries as they interact with formal university policy-making structures for mobility and support. It longitudinally tracks how students' visions of 'new possibilities' are initially articulated, then either captured and integrated or filtered out and faded as they move through institutional channels where students are typically positioned as recipients rather than co-creators. The study uniquely maps the critical junctures and institutional gatekeeping practices that transform grassroots, future-oriented ideas into actionable policy or relegate them to forgotten proposals. This reveals the dynamic tension between emergent student-led innovation and the stabilizing inertia of administrative systems in the post-crisis university.

ideas.title: Disaggregating the Impact of Inquiry-Based Design on High-Stakes Assessment Performance Across Postgraduate Subgroups
ideas.description: This research investigates how inquiry-based instructional design (IID), explicitly framed to enhance performance on high-stakes exams, differentially impacts postgraduate student success when student populations are disaggregated. It moves beyond treating postgraduates as a monolithic group by analyzing how factors such as disciplinary norms (e.g., STEM vs. humanities), the precarity of funding (e.g., self-funded vs. stipend), and family status create distinct 'constraint profiles' that interact with the pedagogy. The study aims to produce a nuanced framework showing which IID strategies are most effective for which subgroups under exam pressure, thereby aligning pedagogical innovation with both equity goals and assessment success.

ideas.title: Localized Ecological Niches as the Primary Unit of Analysis for Educational Systems
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from Ecology and Evolutionary Biology, this idea challenges the assumption that national context is the primary analytical frame for education research. Just as ecologists shifted from analyzing species success based on broad climate zones to studying local 'niches' defined by microclimates and biotic interactions, this research prioritizes city-level or regional governance as the immediate environment shaping student experiences. The analogous solution—developing fine-scale habitat mapping and local adaptation studies—is adapted to create methods for mapping 'educational niches.' The adapted research idea treats schools and their municipal/community contexts (e.g., local funding, district leadership, community partnerships) as the primary unit of analysis. National policy is thus reframed from a deterministic force to one variable filtered through and actively shaped by these localized governance ecosystems, which are the direct drivers of student experiences and outcomes.

ideas.title: Institutional Ethnography of Policy Misalignment: Mapping Administrative Interpretation and Student Exploitation
ideas.description: This research employs institutional ethnography to investigate how university administrators interpret and enact state policies, specifically focusing on the moments where their local interpretations create misalignments with the policy's original intent. Crucially, it then traces how these documented misalignments—whether as loopholes, contradictions, or ambiguous standards—are identified, navigated, and strategically exploited by students and student-facing staff. The study thus maps the complete social circuit from policy text to administrative work to student agency, revealing misalignment not as a simple failure but as a dynamic, generative site of institutional friction and tactical opportunity within the university.

ideas.title: Analyzing II Enactment as a Phenotype: A Comparative Ecology of School Streams
ideas.description: This research adapts a methodology from ecology and evolutionary biology to analyze how school culture and academic stream influence Issue Investigation (II) implementation. The analogous problem—studying how a species' phenotype varies across environmental niches—is solved through comparative field studies mapping traits to specific pressures. Adapted to education, the study will treat II enactment as a pedagogical phenotype and different school/stream contexts as distinct niches. By conducting a multi-site comparative case study, it will quantify traits of II implementation (like inquiry depth and scaffolding) and key niche variables (institutional priorities, resources) to systematically explain variation in II practice, identifying malleable versus fixed elements shaped by the school environment.

ideas.title: Tracing the Evolution of Post-Crisis Imaginaries Using Ecological Succession Models
ideas.description: This research adapts the ecological model of succession to longitudinally track how crisis-induced 'new possibilities' imagined by students materialize or fade. In ecology, succession maps how biological communities re-establish and change after a disturbance like a fire, moving from pioneer species to a climax community. Analogously, the study will identify 'pioneer imaginaries' (the initial, often radical possibilities envisioned right after the crisis), monitor the 'transitional' narratives that compete and hybridize, and document the eventual 'climax imaginaries' that become embedded in student worldviews. The solution involves applying methods from landscape ecology—such as longitudinal plot sampling and identifying facilitative/inhibitory mechanisms—to qualitative and survey data, mapping the conditions (e.g., institutional policies, peer networks, media) that determine which imagined futures thrive, are outcompeted, or become foundational.

ideas.title: Valorized Inquiry: Embedding and Assessing II in the Core Curriculum
ideas.description: This approach integrates inquiry investigation (II) into core academic subjects but structurally separates its assessment from high-stakes summative exams. Students engage in II projects on core curriculum topics, which are formatively assessed against a transparent competency rubric focusing on the inquiry process. These project outcomes then serve as the applied, foundational case material for subsequent exam topics, explicitly linking the II work to exam success without making the project itself a high-stakes test. This system ensures II is perceived as essential, rigorous, and valuable by all stakeholders, as it is both visibly assessed and directly enriches performance in the examined curriculum.

ideas.title: Comparative study of student experiences in other hubs
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from Ecology to the study of international student hubs. In conservation biology, scientists use 'indicator species' to efficiently assess the health of a complex ecosystem. The adapted study will identify analogous 'indicator experiences'—specific, high-impact facets of student life such as housing security, access to mental health services, and professional integration—that serve as measurable proxies for the overall success and policy environment of an education hub. By systematically comparing these focused indicators across countries like Australia, Canada, and Germany, the research will generate more precise, actionable policy insights than a broad, qualitative comparison could provide.

ideas.title: Pandemic as a Natural Experiment in the Longitudinal Development of Place-Based Digital Pedagogy
ideas.description: This study leverages the pandemic's disruption of student mobility and physical place-based learning as a unique natural experiment within a longitudinal framework. It tracks how teacher strategies for implementing Issue Investigation—a curriculum inherently tied to local inquiry—evolved under enforced digitalization, and whether these pandemic-accelerated changes persist or revert as physical mobility returns. The research uniquely examines if the disruption acted as a permanent accelerator for a more hybrid, digitally-augmented form of place-based pedagogy or created a divergent pathway that compromises the curriculum's core experiential goals. This reveals the pandemic's long-term role not just as a disruptor of mobility, but as a potential re-shaper of pedagogical DNA for place-based learning.
